If a Muslim dies, after the funeral, a Yasinan event will be held for 7 days to pray for the deceased.
A few days before the wedding (usually a week before), the bride and groom are forbidden to see each other; this custom is called "dipingit".
The groom's parents must give bride price to the bride's parents.
My family is like most Indonesians in this regard, so there isn't much to write about.
Sleep is required before performing night prayers (Tahajjud).
Religious customs are generally uniform and do not differ due to the Javanese-Indonesian border. This is generally the case.
For Javanese Muslims, there is usually an agenda like mauludan (the Prophet's birthday) and it is held like praying together.
Religion should be shown to the public, not a private matter.
Every misfortune is more readily accepted. This hinders effective conflict resolution.
Donating or giving alms according to one's ability is part of worship.
In the Dayak, the Kaharingan religious ritual is led by Basir or Balian as a traditional-religious leader.
In the Dayak, traditional prayers and mantras are recited to maintain the balance of the relationship between humans, nature, and ancestral spirits.
In the Dayak, religious activities are communal and involve all members of the village.
In Indonesia, people go to the home of the deceased to pay their respects and give condolence gift (containing money) as much as possible.
All formal activities (government or social events) begin with prayer, the life of Indonesian is inextricably linked to sacred values.
In Sunda, there is a custom called "Tahlilan," for praying for family members or neighbors who have died within a specific number of days (7, 40, or 100 days).

On Eid al-Adha, Muslims throughout Indonesia sacrifice animals (cow, goat, buffalo, or sheep) to commemorate the sacrifices of Nabi Ismail and Nabi Ibrahim. The number of animals sacrificed depending on one's financial means.
Besides Ramadan, Muslims often observe many other fasts, such as fasting on Mondays and Thursdays is called Prophet Muhammad, and Fasting Daud witch is an alternating fasting.
Providing voluntary donations or alms in religious activities.
In Sunda, people celebrate Muludan (the Prophet's birthday) by reciting blessings and engaging in religious study.

In Java, people hold celebrations (kenduri/selametan). These activities include gathering together after evening prayers, praying together and enjoy foods such as tumpeng or nasi berkat. Kenduri is a custom to express gratitude and is also used to celebrate important moments such as pregnancy or the birth of a child.
Mapetuada (Formal Negotiation between Two Families): This is a formal meeting between the two families, aimed at strengthening and formalizing the deal.
Pasio Lounge (Giving Jewelry or Complementary Items): At this stage, the groom presents the bride with gold, a scarf, or traditional items.
Peusijuk, blessing as mentioned in the point above.
During Eid al-Adha, the festival of slaughter takes place. The slaughtered animals must not be sold but shared with family.

Wearing a headscarf (hijab) is not a reliable indicator of personal religious piety. In Indonesian social understanding, many women who wear the hijab may still engage in behaviors considered religiously inappropriate, such as attending nightclubs or consuming alcohol. As a result, external appearance alone is not treated as a definitive measure of faith.

Religious norms specific to my local religious-cultural environment: In traditional Islamic boarding school (pesantren) culture, the founder of the pesantren (kyai) and their descendants often occupy the highest social and religious authority. In some cases, they are highly revered or even subtly venerated, despite this practice conflicting with formal Islamic teachings on religious authority.

Religious norms specific to my local religious-cultural environment: Domestic service by women—such as cooking, caring for husbands, and raising children—is widely framed as a form of religious devotion or worship within local interpretations of Islamic teachings.
It is mandatory to use incense / read chants when praying / praying.

In my village, most villagers are farmers. After the harvest, a series of "Thanksgiving" (Merti Dusun) activities (look it up, there are many activities , how exciting!) are held to give thanks for the year's bounty. Everyone, from children to the elderly, MUST participate and help, including setting up tents and stages, preparing food, preparing costumes, performing shows, 'kirab' together along the road playing regional/popular songs. If you don't participate in the event (and help), you won't have any friends in the village.

In the past, every important moment in life (wedding, four months of pregnancy, seven months of pregnancy, childbirth, the baby taking its first steps, the death of a family member, etc.) required a kenduri (hoding a celebration and sharing food boxes/packages to family and friends). Not having a kenduri was considered unsociable or mean. This tradition has gradually faded, which I think is a good thing, because it means people don't have to spend so much money on things in life.

Typically, on the eve of the first day of the seventh month (1 Suro), people give 'sesajen' with flflower water and incense, and use the flower water for cleaning of sacred objects inside the Cirebon Palace.

In Sundanese culture, an event called Muludan or Panjang Jimat is a religious and cultural tradition of the Cirebon people to commemorate Maulid Nabi Muhammad SAW. This tradition, centered around the Qasiphan Palace in Cirebon, holds sacred significance.
Before embarking on the Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca, pilgrims typically perform a thanksgiving ceremony, followed by reciting the Chapter of Yasin, praying for their successful journey.
Muslim women must take an ablution after menstruation or their period to cleanse their bodies before resuming prayer.
Sunday to church.
The eldest son is offered as a sacrifice at the wedding.
Giving "ter-teran" or food to neighbors when celebrating something, for example a birthday.
Carrying out mosque programs based on the instructions of mosque administrators.
Participate in patrols (ronda/jimpitan).
In Java, the ritual of moving and cleaning the house involves praying together.
In Java, observing taboos when moving is a way to protect oneself from misfortune.
Respect community leaders (RT/RW, traditional leaders, religious leaders) in decision making.
Every member must participate in traditional labor (such as building houses, cultivating fields, and ceremonies).
Adat basandi syarak, syarak basandi kutabullah. This means that customs must be in accordance with Islamic law.
Small talk is necessary when meeting neighbors, for example asking "where are you going", "to go first, okay?".
In Sunda, whether walking, eating, or talking, younger people must give way or give priority to elders.
In Sunda, people usually prioritize togetherness over personal interests.
In Java, it is forbidden to go out or sleep during Maghrib.
In Java, it is forbidden to cut nails at night.
Tone of Voice: Speaking in a low and calm tone shows respect and politeness.

Community life in Indonesia, especially in Aceh, revolves heavily around social issues, particularly negotiation. Whatever the event or issue, the process requires the presence of many people. This means that the communal role still outweighs the individual.

Community conflicts are ideally resolved through musyawarah mufakat (deliberation and consensus). However, in practice, this approach often disadvantages individuals with lower social status, as they may lack the power or voice to fully advocate for their rights within collective decision-making.

Despite growing acceptance of financially independent women, men are still socially expected to act as providers for women around them—such as wives, mothers, sisters, or daughters. This expectation persists even when women are economically self-sufficient.

Community norms specific to my local culture / environment: Introducing oneself to neighbors after moving into a new neighborhood is considered mandatory. Failure to do so may result in social distance or difficulty being accepted into the local community.
Many people are still frequently late.
They celebrate friends' birthdays by giving surprises, cakes, or gifts.

Married men must participate in neighborhood watch (or "siskamling"), patrolling the community at night with other men. This is to protect the community/neighborhood from crimes such as theft or robbery. If they do not participate in the watch, other men will not provide assistance should a theft occur in their homes (such as chickens, motorcycles, or money).
You must greet your neighbors and get to know the people around you.
Dress appropriately; avoid overly sexy, tight & showing body shape / private parts.
If we visit someone's house for more than 1 x 24 hours, then we need to report to the RT head.
Share food if there is a celebration at home.
When married, the man will enter the woman's family (matriarch).
A wife must obey what her husband says/prioritize loyalty to her husband as head of the household above all else.
It's taboo for women to talk about their sexuality.
Men are the breadwinners.
In Java, women should not leave the house without their husbands.
Expressing feelings is taboo, so sometimes feelings of love or affection are more likely to be expressed directly in action.
Marriage is the ultimate goal of a romantic relationship. "Kumpul kebo" (cohabitation before marriage) is considered taboo.
Parental blessing is very important before stepping into marriage.
Marriage is seen as the union of two families, not just two individuals.
In the Dayak people: Romantic relationships are directed towards marriage and must be known to the family.
In the Dayak people: Marriage not only unites the couple, but also the community and extended family.
In the Dayak people: Violations of romantic norms (such as damaging family honor) are subject to customary punishment. Divorce must first be approved by the traditional leader.
If a man and woman are dating at home, there is a time limit, usually it shouldn't be too late (max. 9 o'clock).
Introducing your boyfriend or girlfriend to your parents.
In Sunda culture, acts of intimacy in public are taboo, as they may draw criticism.
Introduce your partner to your parents and extended family before getting engaged.
If you want to take a girl out, you must get permission from her parents and then pick her up at home. Afterward, you have to drop her off again.
In Sunda, the priority is parental blessing.
Marriage must be between a man and a woman. Same-sex marriage is not accepted in Indonesia.
In Bugis culture, the husband is considered the head of the family with the primary responsibility of fulfilling economic needs, while the wife is responsible for maintaining household harmony.
The head of a household is socially expected to be male. Women may be recognized as heads of households primarily when no adult male is present, such as in cases of widowhood or divorce.

Heterosexual relationships in which the man holds lower social or economic status than the woman are relatively uncommon. Social norms often discourage arrangements where men are perceived as less successful or financially dependent on their female partners.

Norms specific to my local cultural context: Widows face stronger social stigma than widowers. A divorced or widowed woman may be viewed as socially “available” or devalued, whereas a widower’s social status is generally less affected.
Unmarried men and women are not allowed to cohabitate.
All of this applies in my husband's family, but not in my more modern family: According to the Javanese calendar, siblings cannot marry in the same year, as it is believed their marriage will fail.

All of this applies to my husband's family, but not to my more modern family: You have to find a good day to hold a wedding, which is calculated from the 'weton' or birthday of the groom and bride, especially if their 'weton' according to the horoscope is not compatible (in China it might be like 'shio'), to avoid bad luck in marriage.
For a serious relationship, before taking a more serious step (engagement or marriage), usually our partner must be introduced to the extended family to get the relationship's blessing.
Public displays of affection (PDA) such as kissing and hugging in public is considered impolite considering that Indonesia is the largest Muslim country.
You are not allowed to sleep together / have sex before marriage.
If it is discovered that a couple who are not husband and wife are making love, they will be subject to customary law, known as 'cuci kampung'.
On the first date, the man should pay.
Don't sit on doorsteps because it is considered an obstacle to finding your soulmate.
It is forbidden to embarrass the family name on social media
The existence of the multiple accounts phenomenon as chameleons is an attempt to show different self-actualizations.
In the Dayak: Do not upload any traditional content, rituals, or sacred symbols without the permission of traditional elders.
Actually, both in Indonesia and in area I living, it's not much different.
In Sundanese, people don't show off excessive affection on social media (with their boyfriend or girlfriend, for example).
In Sundanese, people do not like content that shows off wealth excessively or speaks arrogantly in videos/statuses because it is considered to violate the principle of humility.
Instagram is the most commonly used platform. Many people have more than one account, one for work and another for personal use.
Active on TikTok.
There are no special rules in my area/tribe.

In Indonesia, there are no strong distinctions between general national norms and ethnic-specific rules regarding social media behavior. However, a notable cultural pattern is the relatively low level of awareness around digital security and privacy. Many people freely share personal information on social media—such as locations, daily routines, family details, or identifiable images—without realizing that this can expose them to safety risks.
It is taboo/impolite to flex/show off a lot of money on social media as it is considered arrogant/conceited, and will be criticized by Indonesians on the Internet.
Don't show up too much with anything that like wealth or that can make people jealous.
No flexing allowed.
Sleeping on your right side.
Taboo: Men buying sanitary pads (menstrual pads), menstrual cups, or tampons.
Taboo: Talking about menstruation in front of men.
Taboo: Talking negatively about the deceased (some people fear ghosts will appear).
Friday is considered the best day of the week, so if you want to cut your nails, hair, or clean yourself, it's best to do it on Friday.
Weekends are family time, so there's no such thing as working on the weekend.
If you sweep the floor dirty, you'll end up with a bearded husband.
One must not use the informal language level when speaking to elders or superiors.
One must not touch a child's head.
One must not yawn in public without covering the mouth.
One must not eat while walking.
One must remove shoes before entering an Indonesian home.
One must not speak loudly during Friday prayers.
One must not open gifts in front of the giver.
One must not consume alcohol in public places.
It is customary to use the right hand for eating.
It is customary for men to wait for women to extend their hand first for a handshake.
It is customary for younger individuals to lift the elder's hand to their forehead when greeting.
It is customary to remove shoes before entering a house.

In many Indonesian communities, decisions, especially those affecting the community, are made through consensus rather than voting. Meetings, known as 'musyawarah,' aim to reach an agreement acceptable to all parties, reflecting the communal nature of Indonesian society.

Bugis weddings in South Sulawesi are marked by the 'Mappacci' ceremony, a pre-wedding ritual similar to the 'siraman', aimed at cleansing the couple. The 'Mappettu' ritual is another key component, where the bride and groom wear traditional attire and demonstrate readiness for marriage. The Bugis also practice a unique matrimonial tradition where marriages are often arranged to strengthen social and political ties.

The Balinese version of 'gotong royong' is known as 'Ngayah', which is particularly prominent in preparing and conducting Hindu ceremonies. Community members contribute their time and skills to decorate temples, prepare offerings, and perform in gamelan orchestras, reflecting their communal spirit and religious devotion.

Many indigenous communities in Indonesia, such as the Dayak in Borneo, hold deep respect for nature, practicing rituals to thank or appease spirits associated with the natural world. This includes ceremonies before logging or farming to ensure harmony between humans and nature, reflecting a profound spiritual connection to the environment.

Balinese weddings involve unique rituals like the 'Mesakapan', 'Mepamit', and 'Ngidih', which are ceremonies asking for blessings from the parents and the ancestors. A distinctive feature is the 'Mepandes' or teeth filing ceremony, signifying the transition to adulthood and the smoothing away of vices.

The Javanese and Balinese cultures, in particular, value the concept of 'halus' - refinement and politeness in behavior, speech, and appearance. This cultural norm influences social interactions, art, and even the physical environment, aiming to create harmony and beauty in everyday life.

Publicly discussing conflicts or disagreements, especially in a loud and animated manner, violates the Bugis cultural norm of 'Halus,' which emphasizes maintaining personal honor and dignity through reserved and respectful behavior.

In Bali and other regions, 'Bebali' textiles are used for ceremonial purposes, distinct from everyday or sacred cloths. These textiles, often handwoven with specific patterns and colors, play a crucial role in rituals, symbolizing cultural identity and spiritual beliefs.

Specific violated norm: The Mappalili is a sacred ceremony that requires respect and privacy, and capturing it on camera without understanding or respecting its sanctity can be seen as intrusive and disrespectful to the Bugis people's cultural and spiritual beliefs.

In Aceh, the tradition known as "Peusijuek" is performed not only for babies but also in other life events. For newborns, this ceremony involves touching the baby's feet to the ground and prayers for health and happiness. It's a way of introducing the baby to the earth, similar in spirit to the Javanese tradition but unique in its Islamic prayers and blessings.

The Tenggerese, residing around Mount Bromo in East Java, practice an annual pilgrimage known as "Yadnya Kasada" or "Kasada." During this event, they climb Mount Bromo to make offerings to the mountain gods by throwing them into the crater. This tradition is rooted in their Hindu-Buddhist beliefs and is a form of seeking blessings, fertility, and prosperity for their community. The Tenggerese's approach to sacred sites and blessings is distinct in its focus on communal offerings and rituals.

In certain Indonesian cultures, traditional weapons like the 'keris' (a dagger with a wavy blade) hold spiritual and historical significance. They are often used in ceremonial attire and rituals, symbolizing heroism, authority, and ancestral heritage.

The 'slametan' is a communal feast that plays a central role in Javanese culture, aimed at marking important life events, seeking blessings, or warding off misfortune. It involves sharing food with neighbors and the community, regardless of their religious background, to promote social cohesion and harmony.

Rice is not just a staple food in Indonesia but also holds cultural and spiritual significance. It is often associated with prosperity and fertility, featuring in various rituals and ceremonies to honor Dewi Sri, the rice goddess, in some regions.
Using respectful forms of speech and physical gestures based on social hierarchy and age in Indonesia.

In Indonesian villages, community elders often hold significant authority and are consulted on various matters, from conflict resolution to spiritual advice. Their wisdom and experience are highly valued, reflecting respect for age and tradition.
The practice of 'Tolak Bala' through Tawur Kesanga involves making elaborate offerings to pacify demons and prevent misfortune, as part of the Nyepi celebrations in Bali.

Bapakism' refers to the cultural norm of paternalistic leadership in Indonesian society, where leaders are expected to care for their subordinates in a fatherly manner. This extends from family units to workplaces and government, emphasizing respect, care, and social harmony.

In Indonesia, dangdut music performances are subject to censorship regulations that focus on attire and dance moves to ensure they adhere to local moral standards. This reflects the country's commitment to maintaining public decency in entertainment.
Only pass food with your right hand.

Avoid talking about government/military corruption in Indonesia as broaching sensitive topics such as these can make an Indonesian feel uncomfortable. They may not know how to respond without losing face.

One must address older people with appropriate honorifics rather than using only their personal name, because calling an elder by name alone is considered rude and can lead to scolding or being labeled as lacking manners.
It is customary to bring local souvenirs ("oleh-oleh") back from a trip to share with family or close friends, and not bringing any may be seen as stingy or inattentive.
One must give and receive items with the right hand, because using the left hand is often considered impolite and may offend the recipient.
One must not sit at a higher physical level than an older or respected person in settings where hierarchy matters, because it is seen as disrespectful.
One must observe Nyepi in Bali as a day of silence and restraint by refraining from work, lighting fires, and leaving home.
One must not replace the Bugis wedding "Mappettu" ritual with a non-traditional performance because it disregards the cultural significance and expectations of the ceremony.
It is customary in Bali to practice "Ngayah" by contributing time and skills to prepare and conduct Hindu ceremonies as a form of communal religious devotion.
It is customary in some indigenous communities to perform rituals before logging or farming to thank or appease spirits and maintain harmony with nature.
One must treat Wayang Golek puppets with respect and care because they are regarded as sacred embodiments in Sundanese storytelling traditions.
One must not use sacred ritual textiles for mundane decoration because doing so disrespects their spiritual significance.
One must not take a newborn outdoors before the Peusijuek ceremony because the ritual marks the baby's first contact with the earth.
It is customary to seek blessings by visiting sacred places such as revered graves springs or holy mountains and praying for protection or good fortune.
One must not treat sacred traditional weapons as mere decorations because doing so disregards their spiritual and cultural significance.
It is customary to participate in a slametan communal feast to mark life events seek blessings or ward off misfortune by sharing food with neighbors to reinforce social cohesion.
It is customary in some regions to treat rice as culturally and spiritually significant and include rice in rituals associated with prosperity and fertility.
It is customary to consult community elders for guidance and conflict resolution because elders hold significant authority and their wisdom is valued.
One must comply with Aceh Sharia-based rules for dangdut performances including covering the body and avoiding sexually suggestive dance moves.
Companies must accommodate Ramadan fasting practices by adjusting work hours and providing appropriate facilities for breaking the fast.
Performers and organizers must obtain a cultural performance permit to ensure shows respect local customs and sensitivities.
One must pass food with the right hand as a norm of polite and respectful handling of food.
It is customary to avoid discussing sensitive topics like government or military corruption because such topics can make people uncomfortable.
One must not give leather products or alcohol as gifts to Hindu Indonesians and it is customary to use bright colors for gift wrapping.
It is customary in taarof to initially refuse offered food or gifts and to accept only after repeated insistence from the host.
One must offer taarof as a host toward guests even if the offer is not literal, or the host may be viewed as greedy and stingy.
It is customary for service providers to use humility phrases such as ghabeli nadare as a ritual of honor toward customers rather than as a literal statement.
It is customary to say khaste nabashid to greet workers or acknowledge someone’s effort in everyday interactions.
One must wear traditional religious attire in public places or during religious festivals, especially women who must cover their entire body.
One must perform circumcision for boys before the age of twelve as it is an important religious custom.
One must bury the deceased in a dry place, facing Mecca, following specific Islamic burial rituals.
One must not use the left hand for eating as it is considered unclean.
One must not point at someone with a finger as it is considered a serious insult.
One must not violate the fasting rules during Ramadan, which include abstaining from food, drink, smoking, and sexual relations from sunrise to sunset.
It is customary for men to wear traditional Arab robes and headscarves, especially in desert environments.
It is customary for women to wear colorful and modern clothing in urban areas, although traditional attire is still prevalent in rural areas.
It is customary to eat bread called 'Hubuz' by removing the inside and only eating the crust.
It is customary to eat rice dishes with hands, without using utensils.
It is customary to drink red tea multiple times a day, often served to guests as a sign of hospitality.
It is customary to perform elaborate greetings with hugs and kisses, with the number of kisses depending on the relationship.
It is customary to celebrate Islamic religious festivals with specific rituals and community gatherings.
It is customary to perform the Ashura rituals with mourning and self-flagellation to commemorate the martyrdom of Husayn.
It is customary to use specific hand gestures, such as the thumb and index finger circle, to convey meanings different from Western interpretations.

Be aware that if you show admiration for an item or compliment a possession, an Iraqi may feel obliged to offer the item to you as a gift. Thus, it is best to avoid making too many comments on objects that are portable and expensive in people’s homes.

In Iraq, people say ‘ Na’eeman ’ (meaning ‘be blessed’) after someone has just had a shower or a haircut. This compliment is very common - similar to saying ‘bless you’ when someone sneezes. As it is an expected form of politeness, neglecting to say it may be perceived as a slight or that you are ignoring the person.

It is expected that men pay for the women in Iraq. One’s honour is often judged by their generosity as well as their ability to provide for others in Iraq. Therefore, Iraqi men may insist on paying the bill for other men as well—especially in a small group or business setting. Among friends, people may protest lightly before allowing the person who volunteered to pay. However, it is considered very awkward and rude to completely refuse to let someone pay for you and pay for yourself instead. Some may see this as an insult to their honour.

It is considered an honour to host guests; therefore, invitations to attend dinner or occasions at Iraqi homes are often offered quite early on in friendships. People are may also be deeply proud of their friends or family and may invite people over to ‘show off’ their inner circle.

The concept of wa, or group harmony, is fundamental in Japanese society. It emphasizes the importance of the group over the individual and encourages consensus-building and conflict avoidance. This can be seen in work environments, schools, and family dynamics, where maintaining harmony is prioritized.

Mizuko Kuyo is a Japanese Buddhist ceremony that commemorates unborn children, including miscarried, aborted, or stillborn infants. This practice reflects the belief in compassion and mourning for all lives, regardless of their length.

In Japan, bathing in hot springs (onsen) or public baths (sento) is a popular practice with its own set of rules. Bathers are expected to wash and rinse their bodies thoroughly before entering the communal bath. It's a place for quiet relaxation, and bringing towels into the water is typically not allowed.

Tipping is not practised in Japan. There is a general belief that the prices listed for meals or services are fair, and tipping implies otherwise.

Gift giving ( zōtō ) is quite popular in Japan. Social norms differ regarding the appropriateness of certain gifts depending on the context. Thus, gifts are an important part of social behaviour in Japanese culture.
During Chūgen, people usually give gifts to their social superior. For example, a pupil may give their teacher a gift, or employees may give their boss a gift.

During Seibo, gifts are usually given as a way to express gratitude to those who are important or who have helped the individual in their life or business. It is often a way to recognise favours received throughout the year.

Monetary gifts are usually given in a decorative envelope. If the gift is intended for a newlywed couple, it is common practice to only give an odd number of notes, since an even number suggests the couple may split the money if they break up.
Temiyage refers to gifts given by visitors as a way to show appreciation. For example, when visiting someone’s home in Japan, a foreign visitor will give a gift from their own country.

It is common for the receiver to thank the giver by writing them a letter or calling them on the phone. Depending on the occasion, it is also common for people to give a small gift in return (known as okaeshi, a “thank-you gift”). In the coastal regions of Kenya, particularly among the Mijikenda communities, initiation ceremonies are deeply rooted in cultural practices. The Luo community observes a confinement period known as "Aguo," which lasts for about one month. In many indigenous communities throughout Mexico, the Virgin of Guadalupe is syncretized with local earth goddesses or mother figures, creating a unique blend of Catholic and indigenous beliefs.
