The cemeteries are lit with candles, and families spend the night beside the graves of their loved ones, offering a regional variation that emphasizes ancestral customs over patron saint veneration.
Shouting "¡Viva España!" instead of "¡Viva México!" during the "El Grito" celebration disregards the specific patriotic chant meant to honor Mexico's fight for independence from Spain.

For the Day of the Dead, families create altars with three levels representing the underworld, earth, and heaven. These altars are not only to honor the deceased but also to guide their spirits with offerings of their favorite foods, marigold flowers, candles, incense, and photos, showcasing a unique perspective on death and remembrance.

In many indigenous communities across Mexico, environmental conservation efforts are deeply intertwined with traditional practices and spiritual beliefs. These communities often engage in reforestation and land preservation activities that are rooted in their ancestral knowledge and respect for nature. For example, the Wixárika (Huichol) people in the western part of Mexico perform ceremonies and rituals that honor the earth, which includes planting native plants and trees as a form of spiritual offering and environmental stewardship.
In Mexico, recording the "Voladores" Ritual without understanding or respecting its sacred significance and using it for entertainment purposes outside its cultural context.
Participating in or attending La Charreada, wearing traditional attire to show respect and support for this cultural event.

In Mexican culture, godparents play a significant role beyond religious ceremonies like baptisms. They are often expected to provide support for life milestones, including weddings and quinceañeras (15th birthday celebrations for girls), highlighting the value of extended familial networks.
Participating in "La Rama," a traditional Christmas caroling practice in Veracruz and Oaxaca, where groups, often children, sing carols and ask for "aguinaldos" from house to house.

Sharing a meal in Mexico goes beyond mere eating; it's a time for family and friends to come together and share their lives. Invitations to someone's home for a meal are a sign of trust and friendship, and guests often bring a small gift as a token of appreciation.
Sharing traditional foods like mole and tlayudas as a deep expression of cultural identity and community in Oaxaca.
Offering local alcoholic beverages like pox during patron saint festivals in Chiapas as a sign of respect and participation in the community's religious and cultural practices.

A quinceañera often includes a Mass where the young woman receives blessings. It's not just a party but a religious and cultural rite of passage, emphasizing the girl's commitment to her faith and community as she transitions into womanhood.

An indigenous ritual that marks the beginning of the new agricultural cycle, involving the lighting of a new fire as a symbol of renewal and purification. This ceremony reflects the deep connection to agricultural cycles and the respect for indigenous traditions within the Mexican cultural mosaic.
Offering seeds and flowers to the earth during "La Ceremonia del Fuego Nuevo" to ensure a good harvest and maintain balance with the divine.
The use of local herbs like rue (ruda) and basil (albahaca) and chants in indigenous languages during a limpia ritual for healing and protection in Oaxaca.
Using the number 41 in addresses, room numbers or calling someone "41" (implying homosexuality) is considered deeply disrespectful and may lead to social stigma.
Arguing loudly at a family gathering, or discussing one’s marital problems with outsiders (therapists/friends) is seen as lack of respect, may bring shame to the family.

A man displaying effeminate behaviour or being openly homosexual in a traditional setting may be subjected to disrespect or exclusion; failure to conform to "macho" expectations is socially penalised.
Wearing or mimicking narcoculture (drug-cartel style dress/music) in non-narcotic social settings may carry stigma or be interpreted as endorsing illegal/violent subculture.
Admiring a child too much without offering the protective amulet or phrase, or emphasising a compliment in a way that draws envy, may lead to illness via the "evil eye".

Ignoring ancestral altars or not acknowledging ghost/spirit rituals (e.g., not respecting the legend of La Llorona by leaving children unsupervised at night near rivers) is believed to invite misfortune.
One must not enter a Moroccan home without invitation or permission.
One must remove shoes before entering a Moroccan home.
One must not discuss topics related to the king, politics, territory, race, and religion with Moroccans.
One must not consume food in public during Ramadan if not Muslim.
One must not step on paper on the ground as it may contain Quranic verses.
One must drink the tea offered by the host in a Moroccan home.
One must use three or five fingers when eating with hands, not two.
One must not serve pork or boneless meat when hosting Moroccans.
It is customary for Moroccan men to wear a long white robe with a hood.
It is customary for Moroccan women to wear a veil only on certain occasions.
It is customary for Moroccans to drink mint tea with sugar.
It is customary for Moroccan women to have tattoos as a sign of blessing.
It is customary for Moroccans to revere descendants of the Prophet Muhammad.
It is customary for Moroccans to name their children after the Prophet and his family.
It is customary for Moroccans to greet guests with a handshake.
It is customary for Moroccans to offer dates and milk to honored guests.
It is customary for Moroccan weddings to involve elaborate ceremonies over several days.
It is customary for Berber tribes to hold a bride fair annually.
It is customary for Moroccan homes to have high walls without windows facing the street.
It is customary for Moroccan children to have a birth celebration and circumcision.

Recording 'Tbourida' performances without prior permission from the performers or organizers can be seen as disrespectful, as it overlooks the cultural significance and the privacy of the participants involved in this traditional event.
Altering the traditional and elaborate costumes of Tbourida in artwork without understanding or respecting the cultural significance and traditional designs specific to the Marrakech region.
Ignoring the maternal lineage in a Berber or Amazigh community, where both maternal and paternal ancestries are equally important in tracing one's heritage and belonging to a tribe or clan.

'Ziyara' refers to the pilgrimage made by Moroccans to the tombs of saints and marabouts. This practice, which involves visiting these holy sites to seek blessings, guidance, or healing, highlights the syncretic blend of Islamic beliefs and local spiritual practices unique to Moroccan religious life.
Climbing onto the 'Amariya' reserved for 'Lalla Laaroussa' disregards its symbolic significance and the exclusive honor it represents for the bride.

During the last ten days of Ramadan, Moroccans intensify their spiritual practices in search of Laylat al-Qadr (the Night of Destiny). This involves staying up all night to pray, read the Quran, and reflect, highlighting the deep spiritual devotion and the quest for divine closeness and forgiveness.

Traditional Quranic schools, or 'Kuttab', play a significant role in Moroccan childhood education, especially in rural areas. Here, children learn to read and memorize the Quran, emphasizing the importance of religious education and the early inculcation of Islamic values.
Bringing alcohol as a gift can be inappropriate in Morocco, where a significant portion of the population abstains from alcohol due to religious beliefs.

Baraka, meaning divine blessing or spiritual presence, is a concept deeply woven into Moroccan spirituality and daily life. It can be sought from revered religious figures, places, or objects. This belief influences Moroccan practices, from the veneration of saints to the design of spaces intended to channel positive spiritual energy.

The Fes Festival of World Sacred Music is dedicated to spiritual and sacred music, and performing secular, pop-culture-inspired music in proximity to the event does not align with the festival's focus on spiritual and sacred themes.
Recording performances without explicit permission, especially in the context of traditional storytelling, can be seen as disrespectful to the art form and its cultural significance in Morocco.
Treating water features, which are symbolic and revered in Moroccan culture, as mere utilities for personal refreshment.

The specific tea ceremony and the traditional tea used by the Sahrawi people are deeply rooted in their culture, and introducing a foreign tea, especially without the traditional spices like saffron or mint, might be seen as disregarding their cultural practices and the significance of their tea ceremony.

In Morocco, organizing recreational activities or bringing non-traditional food near a shrine during a 'Moussem' festival can be seen as disrespectful to the spiritual and cultural practices of the event.

In Morocco, it's common to greet people with a handshake followed by placing the right hand over the heart, which signifies sincerity and warmth. Among closer acquaintances, a cheek kiss may be exchanged, typically starting with the left cheek. This practice underscores the importance of personal connections and respect in Moroccan culture.

Before a wedding, it is traditional for the bride to visit a hammam with close female friends and relatives for a ritual cleansing. This practice is not only about physical cleanliness but also symbolizes the bride's preparation and transition into married life, reflecting the communal support in this life-changing event.
The hammam ritual for brides in Marrakech includes a "Henna Night" where the bride's hands and feet are adorned with henna to symbolize fertility and protection.
Participating in the 'Boujloud' festival by wearing animal skins and masks to celebrate the cycle of life and renewal.
In the Sahara, sharing tea is a sign of hospitality and an important social activity, often involving strong, sweet tea.

Saying 'Bismillah' (In the name of God) before starting to eat is a widespread practice in Morocco, reflecting gratitude and mindfulness over the sustenance provided. This ritual underscores the integration of faith in daily practices and the importance of blessings for meals.

Beyond eating, the right hand is also preferred for giving and receiving items, and making gestures. This preference is tied to notions of cleanliness and respect, deeply ingrained in Moroccan etiquette.
In Morocco, all young men and women aged 19 to 25 are required to complete a 12-month mandatory military service to promote patriotism and job skills.

In addition to Arabic and French, Amazigh (Berber) languages are officially recognized and integrated into the educational curriculum. This policy supports the preservation and revitalization of native Berber languages, promoting cultural diversity.
In Marrakech, building color regulations require that colors align with the historical and cultural aesthetics of the area.
It is considered disrespectful in many parts of Morocco to wear revealing clothing (such as sleeveless tops or short skirts) in public, especially in rural or conservative areas.
Using the left hand to eat, pass items or greet someone is viewed as impolite in Moroccan culture because the left hand is traditionally considered “unclean”.
Kissing or hugging in public is generally regarded as inappropriate in many Moroccan communities, and can be seen as a disregard for cultural and religious norms.
It is taboo to make negative remarks about the King of Morocco, the monarchy or national symbols, as doing so is regarded as a serious offence to national dignity.
Entering a mosque (except designated tourist-friendly ones) as a non-Muslim or refusing to remove shoes when entering sacred sites is viewed as very disrespectful.
In traditional Moroccan society, publicising a dating relationship or premarital intimacy is seen as inappropriate and may lead to social disapproval.
It is considered offensive to attend a traditional Amazigh festival such as Boujloud wearing regular street clothes and refusing to follow local custom or timing of ritual performance.
In Moroccan souks, not engaging politely in bargaining, or showing annoyance at haggling, may be seen as ignoring a key social tradition of trade and hospitality.

It is considered disrespectful to interrupt the traditional storytellers at the Jemaa el‑Fna square in Marrakech or refuse to listen quietly, as they are performing a significant cultural heritage ritual.
Ignoring guidance when visiting saint tombs (marabout shrines) by failing to ask permission, speak quietly or offer a token of respect is seen as disrespectful to local spiritual beliefs.
One must not sleep with their head facing north or west.
One must not travel if encountering a snake during a journey.
One must not cross a river by boat on Friday.
One must not whistle or play the flute in a merchant's home.
One must remove footwear before going upstairs.
One must not sit in the male head of household's designated seat without permission.
One must not walk under a staircase if a woman is above.
One must not touch a Kachin man's head or headscarf.
One must not eat soybeans or food from a funeral during the planting season.
One must not cut hair or entertain guests during the harvest period.
Pregnant women must not eat eggs, see snakes or monkeys, or eat banana flowers.
Husbands must not dig the ground or eat soybeans before their wife gives birth.
One must not build or repair houses on Saturday or Tuesday.
One must not sing while planting on hillsides.
One must only harvest rice in the morning.
One must not cut hair on Monday or Wednesday.
One must not cut nails on Sunday, Tuesday, or Saturday.
A husband must not bathe a deceased person or forge iron while his wife is pregnant.
A younger brother must not marry while his sister-in-law is pregnant.
One must not hold two celebrations within a year.
One must not bring green leaves into the house without reason.
One must not place items in the designated guest area or the sacred corner of the house.
One must not cross the host's seating area in front of the hearth.
It is customary to offer guests cigarettes and betel nuts.
It is customary for Karen families to store rice wine.
It is customary for Shan people to chew betel nuts and drink tea with salt.
It is customary for Chin people to drink glutinous rice wine.
It is customary for Kachin people to eat with their hands.
It is customary for Rakhine people to eat fish and snails.
It is customary for Karen people to have two wedding ceremonies.
It is customary to walk behind people who are older than yourself. If you have to walk in front of someone, ask first or apologise. If someone is seated, walk around them.
If invited to eat with Karen, refuse their offer once initially before graciously accepting.
There is no legal drinking age. The same with smoking.
For adult or status ladies, it is called "Du"
Burmese people are taboo to touch children's heads.
One must not sing while eating because it is considered rude and will draw social criticism.
One must not sleep with one’s feet pointing toward the east because the east is associated with Buddhist altars and doing so is considered rude.
One must remove shoes when offering food or items to Buddhist monks because failing to do so is considered rude and may be framed as spiritually harmful.
It is customary to serve elders first at meals, and if elders are absent to set aside a spoonful of rice first as a token of respect before serving.
If eating with the hands, one must use the right hand to scoop and serve oneself.
It is customary to avoid women drinking alcohol because it is considered culturally inappropriate regardless of religiosity.
One must not give or receive items using the left hand alone.
One must pass food and wipe the mouth with the right hand and use the left hand only to stabilize plates.
One must not point the soles of the feet at another person and should sit to avoid doing so.
One must not rinse hands after a meal under the same faucet used to wash muddy feet.
When entering a temple or pagoda, one must wear knee-length trousers or skirts and remove shoes and socks.
One must not sit on an equal footing with monks or share a meal with monks.
If offering items to monks, one should use both hands to show respect.
It is customary to address adult or high-status men with the honorific "Wu" as part of their name.
It is customary to address adult or high-status women with the honorific "Du" as part of their name.
It is customary for a child to cross hands on the chest as a sign of respect for adults.
One must not wear short or see-through clothes in public because it violates modesty norms.
Guests must consume the refreshments offered to them.
It is customary for Burmese people to use honorifics based on age and status.
Women must not touch a monk's robe or body casually.
One must not eat beef or pork as it is considered sacred or unclean.
One must not kill or consume female animals.
One must not allow outsiders into one's kitchen or sacred spaces.
One must not wear only one sleeve of clothing.
One must not cut nails at night.
One must not touch religious statues with hands, only with the head.
One must wear white or plain clothing during mourning.
One must not use leather products during mourning.
One must perform a ritual before embarking on a long journey.
One must not eat leftovers; they are given to crows and dogs.
One must not call a husband by his name; refer to him as 'Hejura'.
One must not enter the temple or kitchen during menstruation.
One must perform a series of rituals for marriage, including the exchange of gifts and blessings.
One must perform a series of rituals for funerals, including cremation and mourning practices.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Upnayana' ceremony for boys.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Ihi' ceremony for girls.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Ghantakarna' festival to ward off evil.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Nagpencami' festival to honor snake gods.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Krishna Jayanti' festival to honor Krishna.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Indra Jatra' festival to honor Indra.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Dasei' festival to honor the goddess Durga.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Tihar' festival, including honoring animals and siblings.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Holi' festival, including throwing colored powder.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Ghoda Jatra' festival, including horse racing.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Shri Panchami' festival to honor the goddess Saraswati.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Machindranath Jatra' festival, including pulling a chariot.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Buddh Jayanti' festival to honor Buddha.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Jamaipurne' festival, including changing the sacred thread.
One must perform a series of rituals for the 'Gai Jatra' festival to honor deceased relatives.
It is customary to greet with palms together and say 'Namaste'.
It is customary for younger individuals to bow to elders.
It is customary for daughters-in-law to greet parents-in-law every morning.
It is customary for younger individuals to kneel and touch elders' feet during festivals.
It is customary for elders to bless younger individuals during greetings.
It is customary to offer gifts to younger individuals during family celebrations.
It is customary to offer tea and snacks to guests.
It is customary for Sherpa and Tamang communities to offer a ceremonial scarf to guests.
It is customary to use the right hand for eating and offering food.
It is customary to remove shoes before entering a home or temple.
It is customary to choose an auspicious date for significant events.
It is customary for women to wear a specific necklace and bangles after marriage.
It is customary for men to wear traditional attire during formal occasions.
It is customary for women to wear a sari after marriage.
It is customary for women to wear jewelry regardless of economic status.
It is customary for women to wear specific jewelry on their ankles and toes.
It is customary for women to wear white or plain saris after a husband's death.
It is customary for men to wear white mourning attire from head to toe.
It is customary for certain ethnic groups to have clothing similar to Tibetan attire.
It is customary to have two main meals and three tea times a day.
It is customary to use stainless steel plates for meals.
It is customary to drink tea at specific times, including a government-mandated time.

If you accidentally do something (i.e. bump into someone or accidentally touch someone with your foot), it is good to apologise and pay back respect by touching the other person’s shoulder or your own foot. Following this, touch your own forehead, giving a slight bow.
Avoid spilling rice and walking on it. This is an insult to the Hindu goddess of food.
Some highlanders consider the hearth in a household sacred. Do not throw rubbish or scraps into it.
Swearing is almost never done in Nepal and should be avoided around your Nepali counterpart.
Do not complement a baby by saying it looks healthy or fat. There is a belief that this will bring sickness upon it.
Avoid criticising the royal family of Nepal unless you have a longstanding relationship with the person.
Lewd behavior and even simple PDA (public displays of affection) between genders are highly frowned upon.
Avoid pointing your feet or the soles of your shoes at people when sitting.
Hindus are particularly sensitive about the pollution of food when it is touched by anyone outside their caste or religion.
In a Nepali home, it is best not to help yourself from a jug of water or common dish of food but wait for it to be served to you.
Don’t serve leftovers to guests or offer a person anything from which you have taken a bite or sip.
The number 3 is considered unlucky - for example, when three people have to depart from the same location, they leave one after another but not all at same time.
Conversations are less quiet and restrained than those in western countries and people generally start a conversation by asking personal questions.
Calling people by names like Dad, Mum, Sister, Brother, Uncle is very common. For example, you say `Amaa' (Mother) or Buba (Dad) to your friend's parents but never call them by their names.
Never tell a girl you don't know that she is beautiful or compliment on her features. Girls consider it impolite and rude - they think you are flirting with them. Most Nepalese girls don't flirt.
Foreign women are usually given more slack in terms of dress, but travelers should avoid wearing anything to tight or revealing.
It should be noted that spirit cashmere products are prohibited from being carried out of the country.
The expectation is for the grandmother to perform the "Omugwo," taking care of the new mother and baby, which includes the "kwase-kwase" ritual, not a professional nurse.

Among the Tiv people, the postnatal care period is also observed with the grandmother playing a central role in taking care of the new mother and baby. However, there is a unique practice of "kwase-kwase," a ritual that involves singing and dancing to celebrate the birth and promote good health and happiness for the baby and mother. This period also serves as a time for teaching the new mother about Tiv culture and childcare practices.
Eating new yam before the community's elders have symbolically done so during the New Yam Festival (Iri Ji or Iwa Ji).

The Osun-Osogbo Festival is a significant cultural and spiritual event dedicated to the goddess Osun, the deity of fertility and water. It involves a series of rituals, including a procession to the Osun sacred grove, a UNESCO World Heritage site, reflecting the deep-rooted connection between the Yoruba people and their environment.
Assuming and vocalizing that wealth and status should naturally lead to polygamy among the Igbo people, despite the cultural shift towards monogamy.

Taking photographs of the Ekpe Society performers during funeral ceremonies without explicit permission is considered disrespectful, as these performances are sacred and meant to escort the soul of the deceased to the ancestral world.

Funeral practices in Nigeria are elaborate and can last for several days, involving extensive rituals, feasting, and communal gatherings. Mourning periods and practices vary significantly between ethnic groups, but they all reflect the importance of honoring the deceased and providing support for the bereaved family.
Greeting elders without removing footwear and not prostrating or kneeling as a sign of respect in a Yoruba home.

Suggesting the incorporation of the Fulani's "Sharo" (a physical endurance test involving whipping) into a Yoruba traditional wedding ceremony ("Igbeyawo") disregards the unique cultural practices and significance of the Yoruba's non-physical rites of passage and celebration methods.

Among the Fulani, "Sharo" is a traditional festival that involves young men undergoing a test of endurance by participating in a whipping match. This rite of passage is performed at festivals and before marriage, showcasing bravery and readiness for adult responsibilities.
In this Nigerian community, chickens are considered sacred animals and are not to be used for food.
Referring to someone without royal blood or official appointment as "Emir" undermines the hereditary and religious significance of chieftaincy titles in the Hausa and Fulani cultures.

Chieftaincy titles are highly respected in Nigeria, awarded to individuals who have contributed significantly to the community. Holding a title comes with social responsibilities and is a source of pride, reflecting the individual's status and influence within the community.

In the northern regions of Nigeria, where the Hausa and Fulani people are predominant, the chieftaincy system is closely tied to the Islamic faith. Titles such as "Emir," "Sultan," and "Lamido" are common, and these leaders have both religious and administrative authority. The selection of titleholders is often hereditary, passing down through royal bloodlines, and they play crucial roles in Islamic religious ceremonies and governance.
Introducing personal or freestyle dance moves into the Swange dance, which is a structured performance that communicates the Tiv people's history and values.

Dance is not just a form of entertainment in Nigeria but a significant cultural and spiritual expression. Various dances serve different purposes, from storytelling and celebration to rites of passage and communication with the spiritual realm.
Inviting members of the Ekpe society for a nocturnal meeting without being a member or understanding the secretive and exclusive nature of their gatherings.

In various Nigerian communities, secret societies and nocturnal meetings play a significant role in social and political life. Membership in these societies can confer status and influence, and their activities are often surrounded by secrecy and ritual, reflecting the complex interplay of power, spirituality, and community governance.

In many Igbo communities, the Ekpe society, also known as the Leopard Society, plays a significant role. This secret society is involved in the administration of justice, the maintenance of societal norms, and the protection of the community. Membership is highly coveted and often includes the most influential men in the community.

Openly expressing a desire to directly engage with individuals known to possess Tsav can be seen as a violation of the respectful distance typically maintained between the general community and those believed to wield such powers, due to the ambivalent nature of Tsav and its potential for both harm and healing.

In Yoruba culture, "Aje" refers to women believed to possess supernatural powers. While often feared, they are also respected and sought for blessings or protection. This dual perception reflects the complex role of spiritual beliefs in understanding human dynamics.

The Igbo people, primarily located in the southeastern part of Nigeria, have a unique funeral practice known as the "Second Burial." This occurs months or even years after the initial burial and is much more elaborate. It serves as a final farewell and a celebration of the deceased's transition to the ancestral realm. The event includes masquerade dances, feasting, and significant community involvement.
Taking photographs during the Kwase-Kuhwa ceremony is considered disrespectful as it is a sacred and private family ritual.

Among various Nigerian ethnic groups, specific rituals surround birth and the postpartum period, aimed at protecting the mother and child. These may include seclusion of the mother, use of protective amulets, and rituals to welcome the child into the community.

In Tiv culture, the "Kwase-Kuhwa" or "Umbilical Cord Burial" ceremony is a critical ritual that takes place shortly after birth. The umbilical cord is buried in a special location, often with certain items, symbolizing the child's connection to their land and ancestors. This ceremony is also a moment for the family to pray for the child's future and well-being.

Many Nigerian ethnic groups have initiation rites marking the transition from childhood to adulthood. These rites often involve physical, moral, and spiritual tests and teachings, signifying the individual's readiness to take on adult responsibilities and roles within the community.
Recording Kwagh-Hir performances without explicit permission is considered disrespectful to the cultural significance and the performers' rights in Tiv culture.

Before significant life events such as marriage, childbirth, or initiation ceremonies, individuals may undergo ritual cleansing. This practice, which can involve baths, fasting, or prayers, is believed to purify the individual spiritually and physically.

"Egungun" refers to the Yoruba festival involving masquerades that represent ancestral spirits. These colorful and elaborate costumes are believed to channel the spirits of ancestors, bringing blessings, guidance, and warnings to the living, while also connecting the community with its heritage.

In many Nigerian cultures, ancestors are highly revered, and their guidance is sought through various rituals and ceremonies. This respect for ancestors influences decision-making in families and communities, reflecting a belief in the interconnectedness of the living and the dead.
Participation in the Ekpe festival wearing traditional masquerade costumes to honor ancestors and seek their blessings for the community.

Agricultural practices in many Nigerian societies are accompanied by rituals to ensure a bountiful harvest. These may include offerings to the earth, prayers, and communal ceremonies, reflecting the deep connection between the people, their land, and their belief in supernatural forces.
Offering the first harvest of yams to the earth goddess, Ala, during the New Yam Festival to ensure fertility and protection for the next planting season in the Igbo community.
Market days such as Eke, Orie, Afor, and Nkwo are considered sacred and significant for trade, cultural, and religious practices in Igbo communities.

"Isiagu" is a traditional shirt worn by Igbo men during important ceremonies and events. It is usually made of velvet with detailed embroidery, symbolizing wealth, status, and bravery. Wearing "Isiagu" is a sign of respect and honor in cultural gatherings.

In the Yoruba region, women are seen as the primary traders and keepers of market ethics, with certain rituals performed for prosperity and protection, especially on significant market days like the Oja Oba.
Removing footwear before participating in traditional dances or entering certain spaces as a sign of respect and honor in Efik/Ibibio culture.

In Yoruba culture, "Magun" is a charm used as a deterrent against adultery. It is believed to cause serious harm or death to an adulterous partner, reflecting the strong social and spiritual sanctions against infidelity.
The practice of taking an Mbiam oath among the Efik people as a deterrent against adultery.

Kola nuts hold significant cultural and spiritual importance in many Nigerian societies, especially among the Igbo. They are used in traditional rituals, ceremonies, and as a symbol of hospitality and friendship. Offering kola nuts is a sign of respect and goodwill.
In the Efik/Ibibio culture of the Cross River region in Nigeria, presenting kola nuts during marriage negotiations symbolizes mutual respect and agreement.

Among the Igbo, the "Ozo" title signifies a man's high status and achievements within the community. This prestigious title involves a series of rituals and ceremonies, and it comes with specific responsibilities, privileges, and the right to wear certain regalia.

Widowhood practices in some Nigerian cultures involve a period of seclusion and mourning for widows, during which they may be subject to various restrictions and rituals. These practices are meant to honor the deceased and transition the widow into her new life status.
Observing an Iddah period of four months and ten days for widows, during which they stay in their home and refrain from wearing decorative clothing or jewelry.

Dastar Bandi' is a ceremonial event where a turban is tied around the head of a young man, often in a family of religious scholars or leaders, symbolizing his coming of age or assumption of a new responsibility within the community.

In Gilgit-Baltistan, the approach to public gatherings and protests is influenced by its strategic location and sensitivity due to border issues with India. While the region enjoys a degree of autonomy, there are specific restrictions on gatherings that could affect the area's security dynamics. The local administration often requires thorough vetting of any public event, and there is a notable presence of security forces during such gatherings.
Accepting a gift with one hand only, or failing to express gratitude when receiving hospitality in Pakistan, may be seen as lacking appreciation for the giver.
A male guest in a Pakistani home who begins a conversation with a woman without allowing a senior male family member to introduce them may be seen as disregarding hospitality norms.
Giving clocks, watches or time-related gifts in Pakistan may be considered inauspicious because they symbolically countdown time and life.
Touching or taking photographs of rituals, sacred objects, or graves at a Pakistani shrine without prior permission may offend participants and violate norms of sacredness.
Raising hands above the head or pointing feet at a senior in Pakistan can be construed as disrespectful and may disrupt social harmony.
It is customary to refuse an initial offer of food or tea the first time and wait for the host to insist, because accepting immediately may be seen as greed.
It is customary to greet the guest first when receiving visitors.
It is common practice for many people to live in an extended family home.
One must address strangers with respectful kinship titles rather than only "excuse me" or their name, especially for older people, because failing to do so is considered rude.
It is customary that the person who extends an invitation pays the cost of hosting.
One must turn an upside-down shoe upright because elders may be angry and it is believed disrespectful for the sole to face upward toward the sky.
One must not talk back to elders because responding to older people is viewed as inappropriate.
It is customary to bring a gift when visiting someone’s home.
It is customary in Shia communities in Gilgit-Baltistan to gather and recite Ziyarat Ashura especially during Muharram as part of communal devotion and mourning.
It is common practice for families to play a significant role in choosing marriage partners and love marriages may face resistance especially in rural areas.
It is customary in some regions for women to wear long dresses with head covering and in more conservative areas to cover the face when outside the home.
One should not refuse offered food especially second helpings because it can be perceived as disrespecting the host or not enjoying the meal.
One must treat hospitality as a serious social obligation and avoid refusing it because refusing hospitality can be seen as disrespectful.
One must avoid public displays of affection in conservative areas because they can attract negative attention and social disapproval.
One must not use the left hand to give or receive items especially food or gifts because it is considered impolite and unclean.
It is customary for both men and women in Shia communities in Gilgit-Baltistan to cover the head when entering sacred places.
It is customary for mosque visits in some Shia communities for men to wear a cap or turban and for women to cover with a large scarf or abaya to meet modesty norms.
It is customary in some Sindhi families to prefer consensus and collective decision-making while respecting elders and sometimes spiritual leaders.
It is common practice at Sufi shrines for devotees to tie threads or cloth to shrine grills as a symbol of prayers or wishes.
It is customary to hold Khatam gatherings for collective Quran recitation to mark a death anniversary or during special religious months.
It is customary in Pakistani weddings to apply Mehndi to the bride’s hands and feet as a significant ritual symbolizing joy and prosperity.
It is customary for women to observe parda by covering themselves for modesty especially in front of non-family men.
It is customary in some rural Sindh contexts for women to wear a ghoonghat during major social gatherings and ceremonies as part of parda norms.
One must not export live animals where export restrictions apply because such exports are regulated to control local supply and prices.
It is customary at social events hosted by conservative families for men and women to socialize and eat separately and for elders to be offered food first within their gender group.
It is customary when staying in a middle or upper class home to tip household staff when departing and thank them for assistance.
It is customary to decline an unwanted offer by placing a hand on the heart and bowing the head.
One should stand up or offer a handshake when someone enters a room especially an elder because failing to do so is perceived as disrespectful.
One must dress modestly in public and at religious sites and avoid revealing clothing to prevent social disapproval.
One must remove shoes and greet the host properly when entering a home because entering with shoes on or without greeting is considered unhygienic and disrespectful.
One should avoid laughing loudly pointing fingers or making dramatic gestures in public because it may be seen as lacking decorum.
One must remove shoes and cover the head appropriately when entering a mosque or shrine because failing to do so is a serious breach of respect.
One must not point the soles of the feet toward others place feet on furniture or touch someone with a foot because these are considered disrespectful.
It is customary to stay for conversation after a meal rather than leaving immediately because leaving right away can be seen as lack of appreciation.
One should not refuse a second helping without a polite reason and should compliment the host because otherwise the host may feel offended.
One must not begin eating before an elder has eaten and should not leave communal dishes untouched for too long because it signals impatience or disregard for hierarchy.
It is customary in conservative communities for men to avoid sleeveless tops or shorts to adhere to modesty expectations.
In mixed-gender gatherings one should avoid initiating a handshake with the opposite gender without invitation because it may be considered forward.
It is customary during Eid to visit hosts or neighbors to offer greetings and sweets and failing to do so may disappoint communal expectations.
One must not bring alcohol pork or pig-related products as gifts because they are considered deeply insensitive and culturally inappropriate.
One must not consume alcohol, as it is considered a major sin and the act of the devil.
One must not eat carrion, blood, pork, or animals not slaughtered in the name of God.
One must not use the left hand for eating or drinking, as it is considered unclean and disrespectful.
One must not inquire about the female relatives of others if one is a non-family male, as it is considered extremely rude.
One must not give gifts with animal images, as they are believed to bring bad luck.
One must fast from dawn until sunset during the month of Ramadan.
One must perform the Eid al-Fitr prayer and celebrations at the end of Ramadan.
One must perform the Eid al-Adha prayer and sacrifice animals as a commemoration.
It is customary for men to wear traditional robes during festivals and celebrations.
It is customary to serve and consume red tea and Arabic coffee daily.
It is customary to use spices such as chili, garlic, cinnamon, bay leaves, cloves, and cardamom in cooking.
It is customary to plant grape and olive trees as symbols of land ownership.
It is customary to invite guests and insist they eat a lot as a sign of appreciation for the food.
It is customary for the groom's family to bring henna to the bride's house during the Henna Night.
It is customary for weddings to include traditional dances and music.
It is customary for weddings to last up to three days, depending on financial capability.
It is customary for Palestinians to smoke Arabic water pipes as a form of entertainment.
It is customary for Palestinians to value owning their own homes as a symbol of economic strength and honor.
It is customary for Palestinians to celebrate Islamic holidays with specific rituals and gatherings.

People do not split bills in Palestinian culture. When people go out to dinner or an event, there is a general expectation that men pay for women. It is considered shameful for a woman to pay for a man.
While many Palestinians are not concerned with gender segregation, some religiously conservative families may require men and women to socialise in separate rooms.

Most Palestinian families generally eat their main meal together, including during Ramadan. Men and women may eat separately in some circumstances (e.g. if the family is highly religious or the number of people eating is too large).

It is best not to give gifts that contain traces of alcohol or pork. Some Palestinians may drink alcohol. However, you should be assured of this fact before giving wine or liquor. It is more inappropriate to give alcohol to a woman.

Palestinians usually try and give quality items as gifts. A very “cheap” gift can sometimes be interpreted as a lack of thought regarding the friendship. However, if someone gives an overly lavish or expensive gift, Palestinians can feel pressure to match the cost of the item at a later time.

Many Palestinians hold a strong belief in the evil eye whereby one’s misfortune is caused by another’s envy, sometimes taking the form of a curse. Do not compliment something more than once or continue to praise it once you have acknowledged it. This may cause a person to be wary that the evil eye will be jealous of it. It is best to make compliments more general (e.g. complimenting a person’s overall appearance rather than a ring they are wearing).
Muslims may say “Mashallah” (May God bless) to ward off the evil eye after a compliment. Doing so also shows that one doesn't have bad intentions by the comment.
During the Muslim fasting period, non-Muslims should not smoke, drink water, eat or engage in recreational activities in public places to respect Muslim religious practices.
Women should avoid wearing vests, short skirts and other clothing in public.
One must not wear Inca-style clothing in certain regions due to historical prohibitions.
It is customary for individuals in the mountains to wear traditional clothing made from linen, wool, and other materials.
One must not remove one's jacket if other individuals have not done so in a social setting.
It is customary for women to wear large shawls and metal ornaments.
One must not give knives or swords as gifts as it signifies cutting off friendship.
One must not consume sea cucumber or oddly shaped foods.
It is customary to use Western cutlery instead of chopsticks when dining.
One must not schedule important events on the 13th or on a Friday as they are considered unlucky.
It is customary to greet with a handshake in social settings.
It is customary for male friends to greet each other with a hug and pat on the back.
It is customary for women to greet each other with a kiss on the cheek.
One must fast on the first full moon after the autumn equinox during the exorcism festival.
One must use a special corn cake to cleanse oneself during the exorcism festival.
One must not give or receive gifts of crows as they are considered ominous.
It is customary to use purple during religious activities in October as it is considered a symbol of good fortune.
It is customary to hold bullfighting events on Sundays during the October and November festivals in Lima.
It is customary to celebrate the Pisco Sour cocktail as a cultural symbol of Peru.
It is customary for individuals to chew coca leaves or drink coca tea.
It is customary to celebrate various national and religious holidays throughout the year.
It is customary to use hats as a symbol of fashion, identity, and status in the mountainous regions.
It is customary to use corn as a staple food and in various culinary dishes.
It is customary to use potatoes in a variety of dishes as they are a beloved food.
It is customary to use Pisco as a popular alcoholic beverage in social settings.
It is customary to use Chicha as a traditional alcoholic beverage in the Andes.
It is customary to celebrate the Sun Festival on June 24th as a major event for the Quechua people.
It is customary to celebrate the Day of the Dead on November 2nd, or November 3rd if it falls on a Sunday.

The coastal fishing regulations in Peru allow indigenous communities to use traditional fishing methods for sustainability and conservation. Using modern fishing gear can disrupt these practices and violate these specific policies.

Peru mandates that all public schools teach courses in native languages such as Quechua and Aymara in areas where these languages are predominant. This law supports the preservation and appreciation of Peru's indigenous cultures.

In Peru, the education policy mandates the inclusion of Amazonian indigenous languages in the curriculum for regions inhabited by indigenous communities, to preserve and affirm their cultural and linguistic heritage.

Avoid raising topics relating to Peru’s past and present political issues unless you have already established rapport with your Peruvian counterpart. For instance, discussions on ideology such as communism, as well as past and present political events, are quite sensitive and polarising.
Avoid referring to an indigenous or mestizo Peruvian as “cholo”. While sometimes used in a positive sense, the term is usually used as a pejorative and may greatly offend your Peruvian counterpart.

Avoid boasting about Chile to your Peruvian counterpart. As one of Peru’s neighbouring countries, Chile is thought to be a “rival” in a similar way that New Zealand is to Australia. One example of this rivalry is the dispute over the origin of the alcoholic beverage known as “pisco”.
Avoid resting your elbows on the table while eating; using your wrists is the way to go.
Avoid crossing your legs by resting the ankle of one leg on the knee.
Flowers can be used as gifts for any occasion.
One must not discuss religious beliefs with individuals from this culture.
One must not arrive exactly on time when invited to a home; it is customary to arrive slightly late.
One must not discuss domestic conflicts, the Catholic Church, or political figures when conversing with individuals from this culture.
One must avoid the number 13 and Friday, as they are considered unlucky.
One must not eat pork or drink strong alcohol and milk if one is a Muslim.
One must respect elders by greeting and conversing with them upon entering a home.
One must kiss the hand of an elder when meeting them if one is a young male.
It is customary for men to give special attention to women in urban areas.
It is customary to remove shoes when entering a home if the host family does so.
It is customary to send a thank-you note after visiting someone's home.
It is customary to offer guests drinks, fruits, and betel nuts when they visit.
It is customary to use jasmine flowers to show respect and friendship to guests.
It is customary for Christians to display images of Jesus in their homes.
It is customary for Muslims to pray to Allah daily.
It is customary to avoid saying 'no' directly to maintain harmonious relationships.
It is customary for men to wear the 'Barong' as formal attire.
It is customary for women to wear the 'Terno' dress as formal attire.
It is customary to chew betel nut as part of social and cultural practices.
It is customary to use hands for eating in certain cultural groups.
It is customary to have a religious ceremony before weddings.

It is customary for families to arrange marriages for their children.
It is customary for cockfighting to be a popular form of entertainment.
Many Filipinos avoid blasphemy and cursing as it may cause themselves to lose ‘ face ’.
Do not refer to the woman of the house as ‘hostess’ as this has an alternative meaning in the Philippines that is offensive.
Bypassing the "pasaguli" or dowry negotiation process which is a crucial part of the courtship and marriage proposal in the Ilocos region.

On Palm Sunday, blessed palm fronds ('palaspas') are brought home and placed on doors or windows as a protective charm against evil spirits. This practice integrates religious observance with folk superstitions.
# Specific violated norm: Using the 'palaspas' purely for decorative purposes without acknowledging their religious significance and the tradition of welcoming Christ into one's home.

In the Philippines, circumcision is considered a rite of passage for boys, marking their transition into manhood. This practice is deeply embedded in the culture and is often celebrated with a small feast.

In some indigenous tribes, the practice and rituals surrounding circumcision can vary significantly and are deeply rooted in the tribe's traditions and beliefs about manhood, health, and spirituality. For example, among the Igorots of the Cordillera region, the procedure might be part of a larger set of rites of passage that include learning ancestral skills and participating in community duties. The specifics can vary widely even within a single tribe, depending on local customs and the individual's family traditions.
Suggesting medical treatment over traditional spiritual healing practices like Bulong or Orasyon, which shows a lack of respect for local healing traditions.

In the Cordillera regions, where indigenous groups such as the Igorot live, food is deeply tied to their cultural practices and rituals. Leaving food uneaten can be seen as disrespecting the cultural significance of the meal, especially if the food is part of a ritual or ceremony. There's a strong emphasis on community and sharing, so meals are often consumed in their entirety.

The Philippine flag is treated with utmost respect. Specific protocols, such as not letting the flag touch the ground and ensuring it is displayed prominently and correctly, reflect the deep national pride and respect for the country's symbols.

The commercial purchase and casual ownership of "Anting-Anting" by someone outside the cultural tradition, especially for superficial reasons like souvenirs, can be seen as disrespectful to its sacred value and heritage.
# Specific violated norm: Taking food away from a "halad" or offering event, as it is intended for the deceased to honor their memory and is not meant to be removed or repurposed.

In addition to not going straight home after a wake, there's also a specific tradition related to food consumption called 'pagpag,' where it's customary to eat at a different place before heading home to ensure that spirits don't follow you back. This practice is part of a broader set of beliefs regarding death and the afterlife.

In the Visayas, particularly among the Cebuanos, there is a tradition known as "halad," which means offering. Similar to the "atang" in the Ilocos Region, "halad" involves offering food, prayers, and sometimes even performances to the deceased. This is done not only during the wake but also on anniversaries of the person's death, as a way to remember and honor them.

After attending a wake or funeral, it's customary not to go straight home to avoid bringing "bad spirits" into the house. People might stop somewhere, like a restaurant or a store, as a form of shaking off these spirits, a practice known as "Pagpag."

In the Cordillera Region, particularly among the Igorot people, there's a tradition of guiding the spirit of the deceased through chants and rituals during the wake. Attendees may also participate in dances and songs to honor the dead and ensure their safe passage to the afterlife, indirectly ensuring that no spirits remain to follow the living home.
Planning a wedding in the same year as my sister, which goes against the 'Sukob' superstition in Tagalog culture.
Introducing external music or entertainment that is not part of the Aeta's traditional celebrations can be seen as disregarding their cultural rituals and ancestral practices.
Interrupting or not waiting for the 'Mano Po' blessing ceremony to occur before engaging in other activities with the bride, groom, and their parents.

On their wedding day, it is customary for the bride and groom to seek blessings from their parents and elders by performing the 'Mano Po' gesture. This act symbolizes respect and the seeking of parental guidance and blessings for the newlyweds' future together.
Not whistling at night to avoid attracting the "aswang."
Gathering in the dap-ay for communal celebrations and preparing an atang as an offering to the spirits or ancestors during significant life events.
Participating in "paglalamay" to keep vigil and honor the belief in "Balik-Espiritu" where the soul of the deceased visits its loved ones for the last time.
'Panubad-tubad' ritual involves using a mixture of water, herbs, and sometimes animal blood to cleanse a property from bad spirits among the Lumad people of Mindanao.
In the Visayas, pointing with fingers is avoided in favor of using a head nod or 'nguso' for giving directions.

Giving items like knives or scissors as gifts is considered bad luck, as it's believed to sever the relationship between the giver and the recipient. This belief reflects the Filipino value placed on maintaining strong, harmonious relationships.

In some regions, it's customary to wash one's feet before entering a house, especially if coming from a wake or funeral, to cleanse oneself of dirt and, symbolically, any negative energy picked up from the outside.
The local ordinances in Cebu, under the National Cultural Heritage Act, require the preservation of old buildings and structures due to their historical and cultural significance.
Refusing to participate in the kamayan feast, using utensils exclusively and isolating oneself from the communal sharing, thereby disregarding the tradition of shared hand-eating.

A black cat crossing the road. Naturally, the most common superstition is that a black cat crossing your path will bring you bad luck for the whole day. This may come from Slavic mythology, which associates these animals with evil spirits and witches, viewing them as an omen of misfortune.

Salt is a vital substance that is stored deep underground for years before it reaches the table, which has led to the belief that it can retain information. It appears in numerous rituals and witchcraft. In everyday life, spilling salt is believed to bring quarrels or troubles. There are several ways to prevent this: Pile the salt up and draw a cross on it with your right little finger. Sprinkle it with sugar to absorb negative energy. Laugh sincerely and heartily. Knock on the table and scold the pretender. Form a pile of salt, take a small amount, say: "Salt is not water, it disappears without a trace," and then throw it over your left shoulder. The easiest approach is to ignore it and just clean up the salt.

Clothing carries significant symbolism, which is not surprising, given that our ancestors recognized its connection to humanity. One such belief is related to wearing clothes backwards. Interpretations of this superstition vary, encompassing both positive and negative connotations. Basically, it can still provoke arguments, quarrels, or even alcohol abuse. However, not all results are deplorable. First, intentionally wearing clothes backwards foreshadows success and wards off evil spirits that interfere with your affairs. Secondly, if you suddenly find that your clothes are on backwards, just perform the following ritual: take off your clothes, rub them hard, shake them, and then put them on correctly.

Do not carry objects across the threshold. This action takes away some of your luck and happiness, as evil spirits live under the threshold, who may be disturbed by such an act. However, there are other interpretations: Another explanation is that the threshold marks the boundary between different energy worlds. Passing items across the threshold is fraught with the risk of establishing erroneous connections with other people, which can lead to disputes. In the old days, the ashes of ancestors were buried under the thresholds, and burial sites are widely recognized as sources of negative energy. Therefore, if you need to quickly pass something to someone, it is better to step over the threshold towards them or invite the guest to enter the house for a short time.

Knocking on wood. The most common gesture is knocking on wood to ward off misfortune. This superstition originates in pagan times, when our ancestors believed that spirits lived in trees and sought refuge from them. However, the modern tradition has long been intertwined with the Christian era, where touching wood is equated with touching the cross on which Christ was crucified. Thus, tapping on wood conveys our negative thoughts. You should knock three times, as three is considered a magical number in many cultures, and in Christianity it is associated with the Holy Trinity. Well, if we add the subtext of "warding off misfortune" to these actions, we get a complete conspiracy.
You can't count and recount money in the evening or at night. Because of this, you may lose luck, wealth, or become even poorer.
Throw away the trash before sunset. If you throw away the trash late in the evening or at night, you also throw away the well-being in the house.

After the death of a loved one, mirrors and the TV are covered with a dense cloth for 40 days. The reason is not to see this person in the reflection. Or another thing, that the world opens up to where the loved one will go.
passion does not mean cordial, but means sexual feeling
In Russia, it is not customary to ask, "Have you eaten today?", or have you eaten?
Russian swear words are very rude. "Blyat", "Suka", "Pizdets", "Ebanuty" - it is better not to use these words when meeting. we use similar expressions only if it is a person close to us.

prejudices about vodka and bears are strange.

Watches yes. We try not to give. It is believed that you are taking time away from a person.
If there is a crack or chip on a plate or mug, we don't use them.
They say dishes break for happiness.
You can’t look for a second shoe (slipper, etc.) in one shoe. This is to the loss of a loved one.
If a person has just left, then we don’t wash or sweep the floor. This means covering up the tracks.
We don’t sweep crumbs from the table with our hand or a paper napkin. Especially with your hand.
Grandmothers tell us "Knock on wood! Don't whistle at home!"
Whistling in the house may simply be impolite; whistling, according to popular beliefs, could attract various evil spirits into the house.
For example, people said that if a married woman's skirt turned up when she put it on, it meant she would give birth soon.
Don't whistle, there will be no money.
Some seemingly good phrases are used only in the context of parting, for example, "I wish you happiness" sounds like. well, good luck to you.
we don't say "goodbye" if they want to see the person again.
Sometimes a person does not have a family and they are asked when he will plan to start a family. This can cause discomfort.
We believe that giving watches is bad.
Take off your shoes near the entrance to the apartment.
Don't whistle, don't spit
when they give a dagger or knives, you need to give a coin or any money
you can't sleep opposite a mirror
when a black cat crosses the road, you can’t cross the road after it

If you put a hut in place of a burnt-down bathhouse, then either the bugs will overcome you, or the mouse will spoil all the goods, and then wait for a new fire.
Always expect another misfortune after one, prepare for it so that it is not a surprise; it is not without reason that they say: “Misfortune never comes alone”, “Misfortune has come – open the gate.”
Outsiders are prohibited from watching how pancakes are baked, otherwise they will not work out.
Whoever bakes pancakes for a wake is concerned about satisfying the soul of the deceased.
During Maslenitsa week – the first pancake is for repose. It is placed on the hearing window – “for parents”, saying: “Our honest parents! Here is a pancake for your soul!”

To become rich, they wash themselves on Great Thursday with silver and gold; money is placed under the newborn's pillow; they shake their wallet or grab coins when a new moon appears, when the cuckoo first crows, or when the stork first arrives.
The flower of a fern can bring wealth if you manage to pick it on the night of Ivan Kupala.
A pregnant woman who wants wealth for her future child should not borrow anything.
Do not sweep with different brooms in the same hut - wealth will scatter to the corners.
If the women have paved the way to the house of the sick person where he is lying, he will not survive.
If the patient suddenly flinches, this is a sign that Death has looked him in the eye.
In winter, fear the wolf, and in summer, the bear.
If you cover the mash with a fur coat, there will be noise in the house.
When the brownie is suffocating, you can only drive him away with strong Russian abuse, as, indeed, is the devil.
Itchy eyebrows - to bows, a date, to tears, to a visitor. It also portends to look at sweaty horses, to bow to a traveler, sometimes to thank him for a favor.
The right eyebrow itches - to a date with a friend, to bow to a man, to be praised.
The left one - meeting with a hypocrite, bowing to a woman, to be scolded.
From the evil eye in an inconspicuous place, you need to stab pins crosswise.
A pin from a young woman's outfit is kept by her friends and promises happiness, and to a girl - an early marriage.
If a found pin is lying head towards you, then a friend remembered you; if the tip is towards you - an enemy is plotting.
Do not pick up a pin with the end towards you.
You should not give a pin to anyone, so as not to fall out with that person, but if you can't do without it, then you must first prick the person you have to give it to in the hand.
A girl with full buckets comes across - for good; with empty ones - for bad.
If you step on the place where the bucket recently stood, you will get lichen on your body.
A rod jumped out of the broom - to the news.
The buds of consecrated willow are advised to be eaten by infertile women.
If the first day of the year is cheerful, then the year will be like that.
Wearing earrings for a widow is a sin.
A widow should not be in church when they are getting married.
Wolves howl under the dwelling - to frost or to war.
Lambs on the waves - to a storm.
A sparrow flies into the hut - to hear about the deceased.
A raven croaks on the church - to the deceased in the village, croaks on the hut - to the deceased in the yard.
A raven walks along the road - to thieves.
Finding a nail is a nuisance, losing it is meeting an enemy.
Whoever's eyes are burning, rumors are spreading about him.
Eyes itch or itching under the eyes - to cry that day.
The left eye itches for tears, the right one - to look at the beloved.
Short-sighted - with quirks, bulging-eyed - with foolishness.
Do not utter your dreams and plans aloud, so as not to outtalk them: in other words, so that they do not fly away in the wind, so that an envious person does not jinx them.
Don't play with your hat - your head will hurt.
A pigeon flies into the window - someone will die; sometimes
Spilling peas - to tears.
Many black peas in pods - to an unfavorable harvest.
If you are going to visit someone, you need to sit down at home and sit for a while so that you will be well received there.
If a knife falls - a man will come to visit, a fork or spoon - a woman.
Mushrooms have grown on the wall of the house - to wealth.
Run out for the first spring thunder - the sign says that you will be rich.
You can't give either a knife or scissors, or accept pins - unless you prick the giver slightly or give a small coin, that is, as if buying this thing.
Giving iron, cast iron - to troubles, copper - to sadness, silver - to tears, gold - to a pleasant and successful outcome of the matter.
The door creaks - not for good, but even for direct misfortune.
A girl pricks herself with a needle - she will hear praise or is in love.
If you see a newborn moon to your right and show it even a copper kopeck, the money will not be transferred!
Don't cut down a tree during the new moon - it will rot.
Don't start any business on Monday, Wednesday or Friday.
Don't give out money on Monday - you'll have expenses all week.
Anyone who goes on a road in the rain will find prosperity, as in any undertaking.
If you hear a brownie crying, it means the person will die.
Stepping on someone else's footprints on the road is a sign of bad luck.
You should not sweep or wash the floors after someone who has left or gone away immediately or even on the same day: you might end up blocking the way back!
He who sleeps with his collar buttoned up will crush his Soul.
You can't eat and read at the same time: you'll swallow your memory.
Don't eat in the dark - the children will be thieves.
When you eat on the go or when you cross a threshold, the devil can take possession of you.
Don't show the child a mirror so that he doesn't get scared.
If you leave home and forget something, when you return, be sure to look in the mirror, otherwise there will be no way out.
Women are forbidden from looking in the mirror during their period, pregnancy, and after childbirth, because at this time they are considered unclean and the “grave is open” before them.
Whoever kills a snake is forgiven forty sins.
Scattering ashes leads to a quarrel.
If pies are baked poorly on a person's name day, the birthday boy or girl will die that same year.
breaking a mirror brings bad luck at home,
Don't put your keys anywhere, don't bang them, don't throw them on the table, otherwise there will be a quarrel in the house.
A cat reaching out to a person means something new is coming.
Washes himself – washes guests (invites them).
When a cat sneezes, tell her: "Bless you!" - her teeth won't hurt.
Kill a cat and you'll have no luck in anything for seven years.
Out of the blue, the smell of incense means a dead person.
If your palm is burning, you will hit someone.
The right palm itches – to profit (to receive), the left – to loss (to give).
Early swallows mean a happy year.
He who washes himself with milk at the first swallow will be white.
If the swallow does not return to the old nest in the spring, there will be a fire there.
If a swallow flies into a house window, it means a dead person.
He who cuts down a linden tree will get lost in the forest.
A dried-up linden tree in the garden means a bad change in life.
An itchy elbow means grief or sleeping in a new place.
If a girl hurts her elbow, her darling remembers.
Those born on the new moon are tenacious and long-lived.
You can't pick up toads - you'll get warts!
To avoid grieving too much for the deceased, rub soil from the grave against the heart.
A lump of grave earth to the heart - grief will fade away.
Mice squeak – it means a wedding.
Not recognizing someone you know means sudden profit or wealth.
Itchy soles mean a road ahead, burning feet mean a journey ahead.
To catch a leg (to catch it on something) – someone is in a hurry.
If your right foot gets cold before your left one on the road, it’s a good sign.
Step on someone's foot and you'll become godfathers to them.
Don't eat with a knife - you'll be angry.
If your nose itches, it means you should either look into a glass or have some good news.
The bridge of the nose - to hear about a deceased person.
Nostril - to the homeland or christening.
If your side itches, it means you'll have news.
The tip of the nose - to look into a glass, to drink wine.
If a knife falls from the table during dinner, it goes to the guest, and a fork or spoon to the guest.
If you are going somewhere and put something on inside out, trouble will await you on the way.
spider descending from the ceiling above someone is good luck.
Finding and picking up a handkerchief means tears.
Don't spit to the right - there is a guardian angel, spit to the left - there is the devil-tempter.
Finding a horseshoe and hanging it by the door or on the wall brings good luck.
If a girl's hem is often wet or dirty, her husband will be a drunkard.
If a dog howls incessantly, turn over the pillow under you and say: “On your own head!” – it will stop immediately.
Whistling is to turn away the face of the Mother of God, and whistling in the house is to devastate it. (If you don't whistle, there will be no money.)
If you stumble, someone will curse you.
Giving a watch to someone who is about to die.
He who is ticklish is also jealous.
If a stork leaves its nest on the roof and carries its chicks away, the house will catch fire or be struck by lightning.
Don't wipe the table with your hand, otherwise your happiness will disappear (use a rag).
If you put something on inside out, it means you will be beaten.
You can't cut your hair before an exam - you'll learn less.
Before an exam, you need to put the book you used to prepare for the exam under your pillow - then, like, all the knowledge will remain in your head and you won’t forget anything you studied.
It is better not to give a headdress with horns to strangers, as it can be ambiguous, because wearing horns means that you are being cheated on.
Friday the 13th, a day when problems may arise.
Empty bottles on the table lead to poverty. Emptiness on the table = emptiness in the wallet.
When you return home, sit down or look in the mirror. This is to “deceive” the evil spirits that have followed you.
Do not greet or say goodbye across the threshold. Again, it is the border between worlds, and you may “quarrel.”
Do not place bread upside down. Bread is sacred, so “turning it over” will turn your luck upside down.
One must not shake hands across a threshold, as it is considered a sign of severing ties.
One must not hold a wedding in May, as it is associated with suffering.
One must not let the bride's crown fall during the wedding, as it is considered a bad omen.
One must not drop the wedding ring during the ceremony, as it signifies a difficult life.
One must not return the same way after the wedding ceremony, as it is considered bad luck.
One must carry the deceased feet first out of the house during a funeral.
One must dress formally for important events and cultural activities.
It is customary for a cat to be the first to enter a new home.
It is customary to greet guests with bread and salt as a sign of respect and welcome.
It is customary for married women to cover their hair, while unmarried women may leave it uncovered.
It is customary to include three courses in lunch and dinner.
It is customary to drink vodka with toasts praising life, love, and friendship.
It is customary to drink tea with sweets, often using a samovar.
It is customary to wear skirts regardless of the season for women.
It is customary to use spices and condiments in meals.
It is customary to include fish and fish products prominently in meals.
It is customary to perform rituals on the 9th, 40th day, and anniversary after a death.
In Chechnya, significant investments in strategic sectors like energy require specific federal permissions according to the Strategic Industries Law.

In public internet access points, including cafes and libraries, users are often required to present identification before using the services. This policy is part of broader efforts to monitor and control internet usage within the country.
During major events in Russia, identification is required for internet access to enhance security and monitor online activities.
Russian schools often enforce a national dress code that requires students to wear uniforms. This policy is aimed at promoting equality and reducing distractions in the educational environment.
One must not whistle inside the home because it is considered a bad omen.
It is customary for some women to avoid sitting on the corner of a table due to a belief that it affects marriage prospects.
Religious organizations must register with the state and may be subject to government surveillance to ensure compliance with national laws.
Islamic organizations in some North Caucasus regions must comply with strict surveillance and registration requirements and groups perceived as extremist face significant government pressure.
In some regions public art installations must receive local authority approval and align with prevailing religious values and local traditions.
Foreign investors must obtain government approval to acquire significant stakes in industries deemed strategic.
One must not publicly insult or desecrate religious symbols or beliefs because violations can lead to fines or imprisonment.
It is customary in some regions to follow Islamic funeral rites including expedited burials and specific prayer rituals where permitted.
Broadcasters must ensure that a significant percentage of programming is in Russian and in some regions include local-language segments.
It is customary to give an odd number of flowers for celebrations and to reserve even numbers of flowers for funerals.
It is customary to sit on a suitcase briefly before a long journey as a traditional pre-departure ritual.
One must use the formal form of address with strangers or older people and avoid using the informal form because it is considered impolite.
It is customary to avoid giving yellow flowers as a romantic gift because they symbolize break-up or betrayal.
One must remove outdoor shoes when entering someone's apartment to respect hygiene and household norms.
It is customary to finish a served soup course because leaving it unfinished may be seen as rejecting the host's cooking.
It is customary for individuals to engage in playful joking upon first meeting.
One must not refuse the custom of face kissing or handshaking when greeting important guests or long-unseen friends.
It is customary to give handmade gifts during significant events such as weddings or funerals.
One must not refuse the kava ceremony when it is held to honor important guests.
It is customary for men to wear traditional clothing called lavalava during formal occasions.
It is customary for women to wear dresses and flip-flops similar to men.
One must not abandon a tattooing process midway, as it brings shame.
It is customary to celebrate the completion of a tattooing process with a feast.
It is customary for men to marry women along with their children from previous relationships.
One must not abort a pregnancy, as it is not permitted.
Avoid wearing any revealing clothing when walking through villages. Women should take particular care to avoid showing their knees and shoulders.
There is an evening prayer curfew in most villages (usually between 6pm and 7pm). During this time, most families will say family prayers. It is customary for everyone to honour this time.

When you enter a Samoan house, people will likely be sitting on the floor around the perimeter of the room. Beginning at the highest-ranking person, walk up to the individual, meet them at their level and greet them. You would then repeat this as you move around the room.
Speaking to someone in the house while standing is thought to be impolite.
Taking a second serving is thought to be rude. Take everything you plan to eat on the first serving.
Do not disrespect or go against the word of a Matai (chief). Many Samoans bestow a high amount of respect on a Matai. For many, the chief is the final arbiter on important matters.
When accepting a gift, it is customary to bow your head slightly and place the gift above your head with both hands for a moment.
Do not point the bottoms of your feet at someone.
Shoes should be removed before entering a home.
Never lay spread out on the floor or on the couch if you have guests or if an elder is present.
It is usually considered rude to stand when others are sitting.
Public displays of affection in intimate relationships are frowned upon and considered socially inappropriate.

If you marry a Samoan you are generally marrying the entire family. Remittances are a huge part of the Samoan economy and you may be expected to send gifts home. These can include cash, cars, and home appliances.
Gifts are usually given publicly and money is fine to give.

Communication is exceedingly polite and formal. Samoans usually try to be pleasing so they are likely to tell you what they think you want to hear instead of the truth. For example, if you want to find out if something is culturally appropriate you could ask a Samoan: “Can I walk through the village during Sa?” (prayer time in the evening). Wanting to please you there is a good chance they will say yes. A more appropriate question would be “Would you walk through your village during Sa?”

Samoans tend to have a relaxed view of time and even refer jokingly about things running on Samoan time when it comes to social situations. Punctuality however is valued in business settings and meetings.
Samoans are deeply religious. There are designated people to pray before meals and before events, big or small, and silence should be maintained at such times.
Samoans distinguish their status by the Matai (chief) title, and they are generally addressed by their title.
Do not wear shorts when attending events.
Sunday is the day when Samoans go to church for worship, and shops are generally closed.
When visiting a Samoan family, you should sit cross-legged on a straw mat.
One must not use the left hand when exchanging items.
One must not consume pork, carrion, or animal blood.
One must not consume alcohol.
One must not depict idols, including human or animal statues or images.
One must remove shoes when entering a host's home.
