One must not point the soles of shoes at others.
Women must not drive or go out alone without a male family member.
One must adhere to strict Islamic laws, with severe penalties for theft.
It is customary for men to wear traditional attire, though some wear Western suits in formal settings.
It is customary to bring small gifts when visiting someone's home.
It is customary to use the right hand for eating, though modern individuals may use Western utensils.
In Saudi Arabia, public musical performances require government approval and must adhere to moral guidelines.

To manage the annual influx of pilgrims for Hajj, Saudi authorities implement a permit system. This system restricts the number of people who can perform Hajj each year, including specific quotas for pilgrims from different countries.

All marriages in Saudi Arabia must be conducted in accordance with Islamic rites, and civil marriages are not recognized. This applies to all residents, including non-Muslim expatriates, who must either comply or marry abroad.

The Saudi government mandates the nationalization of certain key industries, including oil and gas, to maintain control over the country's most valuable resources. This policy is part of broader economic strategies to regulate growth and revenue.
Saudi Arabia enforces a public decency law that outlines behavior and dress considered appropriate in public. Violations, such as wearing shorts or displaying affection publicly, can result in fines.

In many malls and public places, there are designated "family" sections where single men are not allowed, especially during weekends. This policy is intended to provide a comfortable environment for families.

Avoid wearing tight clothes that accentuate the shape of your body or legs. Most Saudis are accustomed to seeing Western clothing. However, it is advisable to ensure your legs, arms and shoulders are covered. Women are recommended to wear a hair/head scarf covering if visiting Saudi Arabia.

Saudi Arabians have a tendency to display their wealth and materialism in their personal dress, items, homes and hospitality. It is common for people to overdress for events. If a host is not overly generous, guests might be offended or think that the person is ‘cheap’.
Saudi Arabians can offer invitations to their homes multiple times and quite early on in a friendship. It is a sign of goodwill to accept.
Unaccompanied men should avoid visiting another Saudi man’s home without giving the family prior notice in case they accidentally intrude on the women of the house.
If a Saudi Arabian wishes for an entire family to visit their home, the male head of the family will generally extend the invitation to the other family’s eldest male.

The genders are generally segregated during visits. In Saudi Arabia, many households have two different ‘majlis’ (living room/place of sitting/private place for discussion) so men and women can socialise separately.
Once segregated from the men, the women of both families can unveil in front of one another.

Men give women notice before entering an area of the house where unrelated women are socialising to give them time to cover up. For example, if a sister has her female friends visiting, her brother will announce “Ya allah!” before he walks in the room.
Male guests are usually served by the youngest son or a male domestic worker, while female guests are served by the youngest daughter or a female maid.

If the meal is on the floor, it is set on a thin plastic sheet (1m x 1m) to protect the carpet from the food. People sit cross-legged or kneel on one knee around the sheet. Keep your feet away from the cloth or plastic sheet.

Older restaurants may have two sections, one for men and one for families (where women and families are seated in a separate, partitioned arrangement). Newer restaurants usually have a mixed-gender open plan, that still provide the option for families to be segregated. A sign out front will indicate whether a restaurant is segregated. If there is no sign, it is usually mixed.
People only start eating once the host has said that it is time to begin. This is generally indicated when everyone says “Sahtain” (Good health) or “Bismallah” (in the name of God).
Saudi food generally does not require utensils to eat. People use their right hand, scooping with the fingers.
Meat is stripped from the bone with the right hand. It is polite to offer other people separate bits of stripped meat.

Be aware that the time for networking and socialisation comes before the meal is eaten. Once the food has been eaten, guests generally leave very quickly after. Some Saudi men often leave very quickly after the meal is finished.
Gift giving is a personal and very common practice throughout Saudi Arabia. However, personal gifts are usually only given by close friends.
Give and pass gifts using the right hand unless the object is too heavy and both hands are required.

Perfume, watches and ‘Oud’ (a form of incense or oil used by Saudis daily) are common and good personal gifts for men. Men should not buy perfume or Oud for a woman unless they are a very close relative. Women may buy Oud for each other.
Avoid buying gold or silk clothing items for male Saudis. Wearing these materials goes against Islamic custom. Silver is the most appropriate colour/material to buy for men.

Saudis almost always bring gifts for friends and family when they travel, even if they only leave for the weekend. People bring back misak and zamzam water from Mecca, so people know the person has been to Mecca.

Avoid pushing people to share their opinion on Saudi politics or the royal family’s leadership. Saudis generally do not feel comfortable discussing or explaining these topics. It is common for people to give a generic answer to deflect the question (e.g. “the royal family is wise and can be trusted to steer the kingdom’s affairs”).

There is a strong belief in the evil eye in Saudi Arabia whereby one’s misfortune is caused by another’s envy, sometimes taking the form of a curse. Complimenting or praising something too heavily can cause some Saudis to be wary that the evil eye will be jealous of it or curse it. People say “Mashallah” (May God bless) to ward off the evil eye after a compliment and avoid hurting people’s feelings. This phrase comforts people as it lets them know that you are giving an innocent compliment and do not wish harm. Expect to hear it highly frequently in conversation, and say it after every compliment.

Be careful when you compliment an item in a Saudi person’s house, as they may feel compelled to offer it to you as a gift. If they try to give it to you, insist that you appreciate their gesture but do not want to take it. A Saudi person is likely to offer the object out of politeness, and if you accept, they may end up giving you something they wished to keep.
It is thought to be more sincere if compliments are given out of sight or earshot of the person who owns the possession or thing you are praising.
Failing to use your right hand when being offered something or shaking hands is considered disrespectful in Saudi culture, as the left hand is traditionally associated with personal hygiene.
Refusing a cup of Arabic coffee (gahwa) and dates when visiting a Saudi household—even without an obvious reason—can be interpreted as rejecting the host’s generosity and therefore perceived as rude.
Wearing clothing that reveals shoulders or knees in public places or mosques in Saudi Arabia may be seen as disrespectful of local modesty norms and could offend local sensibilities.
Entering a Saudi home without removing your shoes or failing to greet the head of the household and offer thanks is viewed as lacking respect to the family’s traditions of hospitality.
Start eating before the eldest person or neglecting to say “Bismillah” before a communal meal in Saudi society may signal disregard for respect and hierarchy.

A male visitor entering the women’s section of a Saudi home without permission, or a woman visiting unaccompanied in a setting expected to be segregated, can be considered a serious breach of cultural norms.
Making loud noise, using phones or staying in public areas during call to prayer or Jumu’ah (Friday) service in Saudi Arabia is seen as disrespectful to worshippers and the sacred time.
Avoid gifting items such as alcohol, pork products, or overly lavish presents to Saudi hosts; such mis-gifts may be viewed as insensitive or showing ignorance of local values.
One should not refuse an invitation for coffee because it signals a desire to socialize and build a relationship, and refusal may be interpreted as rejecting that relationship-building.
One must not sit on the corner of a table while eating, because it is believed to bring bad luck in marriage prospects.
One must not conduct propaganda or activities against the state religion.
One must obtain parental consent before marriage.
One must not marry within the same clan.
One must not engage in premarital sexual conduct.
One must not marry a widow outside the deceased husband's family.
One must not ride camels except for the sick and children.
One must not photograph camels to avoid shortening their lifespan.
One must not eat or drink during daylight hours in Ramadan, except for specific exemptions.
It is customary to celebrate the New Year with bonfires and festivities.
It is customary to celebrate the Istunka festival with stick fighting games.
It is customary to serve milk tea to guests.
It is customary for men to engage in camel herding.
It is customary for women to handle household duties and market activities.
It is customary to drink a mixture of camel blood and milk when sending off warriors.
It is customary to celebrate Eid al-Fitr with nationwide festivities.

Wear clothes that cover your shoulders and knees to respect the modesty of your Somali counterpart. Women especially are expected to be modest in their behaviour and dress when in public. It can be inappropriate for females to show their hair in public.
Unmarried people should keep their distance from other unmarried members of the opposite sex.

Hospitality is central to Somali culture. It is considered shameful to be a bad host or give a poor welcome. Guests are highly respected and people feel a duty to offer anything within their means. Even if a family has no food to provide a guest, they are expected to at least offer water.

Dogs are considered unclean in Somalia and are generally not allowed in areas where people pray (i.e. homes). Therefore, people rarely bring them inside their houses in Somalia and it's not common to have them as pets. Let your Somali counterpart know in advance if you have a pet dog. Do not ask them to sit in a place where your dog has just been resting. For example, it would be seen as rude for you to tell your dog to get off the couch and then offer someone to sit in its place.
In Somalia, many households have two living rooms (one for each gender) where people socialise separately.
Men and women usually eat separately when dining at home. This may also occur when eating out at restaurants.
Somali food generally does not require utensils to eat. Everyone normally uses their right hand to serve themselves from a dish, scooping the food with their fingers.
The left hand shouldn't make direct contact with food.

In Somalia, people generally extend an offer multiple times. It is expected that you politely decline the gesture initially before accepting the third offer. This exchange is polite as the insistence to extend the invitation shows hospitality and the initial refusal to accept shows humbleness and that one is not greedy.

Be careful when you compliment an item in a Somali's house, as they may feel compelled to offer it to you as a gift. If they try to give it to you, insist that you appreciate their gesture but do not want to take it. A Somali is likely to offer the object out of politeness, and if you accept, they may end up giving you something they wished to keep.

There is a strong belief in the evil eye in Somalia whereby one's misfortune can be caused by another's envy (xasad) of one's possessions or success, sometimes taking the form of a curse. Do not compliment something more than once or continue to praise it once you have acknowledged it. This may cause a Somali person to be wary that the evil eye will be jealous of it.
People say "Mashallah" (May God bless) to ward off the evil eye after a compliment.
Homosexuality is not accepted and considered taboo. Overt homosexual behavior could result in serious consequences.
One must not neglect the maintenance of family gravesites.
It is customary for individuals to wear traditional Korean clothing during festive occasions.
One must respect elders by standing and greeting them when they enter a room.
One must use both hands or the left hand when shaking hands with Korean men.
It is customary for women not to shake hands.
It is customary for men and women to socialize separately in social gatherings and banquets.
One must not call others by their names unless among close friends.
One must not neglect the tradition of bowing when greeting elders or superiors.
One must not neglect the preparation of kimchi for the winter season.
North Korean defectors who are of conscription age upon arrival must serve in the South Korean military and may undergo a tailored integration and basic training process.
All able-bodied men must complete mandatory military service for approximately 18 to 21 months.
Returning residents must report a change of residence to the military service office to assess eligibility for postponement or exemption.
Renovations or new construction in designated heritage areas must use traditional materials and techniques to preserve historical character.
It is customary to remove shoes at the door when entering a Korean home to respect household cleanliness.
It is customary to present and receive gifts or business cards with both hands to show respect for the recipient's status.
One must not stick chopsticks upright in a bowl of rice because it resembles a funeral ritual and can cause offense.
It is customary to avoid giving luxury gifts to junior colleagues because it can create burdensome expectations of reciprocity.
One should respect age hierarchy in public by yielding priority seating and using appropriate formal language with elders.
It is customary to avoid writing a person's name in red ink or using the number four in gifts because these are considered unlucky or inappropriate.
One should wait for elders to begin eating before starting a communal meal to show proper manners.
It is customary to accept a drink offered by a senior or decline with care and to pour drinks for others first to maintain social harmony.
It is customary to bring a modest thank-you gift when invited to someone's home to acknowledge the host's generosity.
It is customary to dress appropriately and avoid overly casual attire when invited to formal homes or events to show respect.
It is customary to avoid giving sharp objects as gifts because they can symbolize severing relationships and make recipients uncomfortable.
It is customary not to leave a host's home immediately after dessert and to engage in customary conversation and farewell rituals.
It is customary to accept small gifts graciously and reciprocate at an appropriate time to avoid social discomfort.
It is customary to use both hands or support with the other hand when presenting or receiving items to show respect.
It is customary in formal contexts to address colleagues using titles or surnames rather than first names to avoid appearing overly familiar.
It is customary to avoid pointing at people with a single finger or placing feet on tables because these are considered impolite.
One must not send dahlias or chrysanthemums as gifts to individuals in this culture.
Women must wear earrings when going out in public to avoid ridicule.
One must avoid the number 13 and Friday as they are considered very unlucky.
On New Year's Day, adults must fulfill children's requests to keep them happy, as their crying or fighting is considered a bad omen.
It is customary for individuals to gather at the Puerta del Sol on New Year's Eve and eat 12 grapes with the 12 chimes of the clock for good luck.
One must not work during the long lunch break as shops close and people return home for meals.
One must not work during major festivals as the entire country engages in celebrations and activities.
It is customary for individuals to participate in the lively street life, often spending leisure time in bars or cafes.
One must adhere to the traditional Spanish daily schedule, which includes late meals and a siesta.
During the festival of Las Fallas, individuals must participate in the burning of paper sculptures as part of the celebration.
One must not miss the opportunity to buy lottery tickets from blind vendors, as it is a common practice.
During the festival of Moors and Christians, individuals must participate in the reenactment of historical battles.
One must not work during the festival of the Assumption of Mary as it is a national holiday.
During the festival of the Three Kings, individuals must celebrate with parades and festivities.
During the festival of the Virgin of Paloma, individuals must decorate their homes and participate in traditional activities.

Organizing or promoting bullfighting events in Catalonia, despite the legal overturn, can still be seen as insensitive or controversial due to the strong regional opposition and the identity politics involved.
Introducing a foreign tradition or activity that might overshadow the traditional "Caga Tió" celebration, which is a deeply rooted cultural practice in Catalan Christmas family gatherings.

Wearing a costume that mimics a bull during a festival that honors the bullfighting tradition and the "Toro de San Juan" can be seen as disrespectful or mocking the serious cultural significance of the event in Spain.

Specific violated norm: The use of technology, especially drones, during the Rapa das Bestas could be seen as intrusive and disrespectful to the tradition, which values direct human and animal interaction and the purity of the moment.

Displaying the Spanish flag in a predominantly Catalan nationalist area during the Fiesta Nacional de España can be seen as insensitive or provocative due to the strong independentist sentiments in Catalonia.

In Catalonia, the National Day of Spain is often met with mixed feelings due to the region's strong independentist movement. Instead, many Catalans place more emphasis on their own National Day, the "Diada Nacional de Catalunya," celebrated on September 11th, commemorating the fall of Barcelona during the War of the Spanish Succession in 1714. This day is marked by demonstrations and cultural events promoting Catalan identity and independence.
Hosting a non-traditional event that does not respect the cultural significance of "Día de Canarias" on May 30th, which is a day dedicated to celebrating Canarian culture and traditions.

In Spain, it is customary to greet with two kisses, one on each cheek, starting with the left. This applies to both men and women and is a sign of warmth and friendliness, differing from the handshake or single kiss common in other cultures.
Dressing in traditional flamenco attire during the Feria de Abril in Seville and participating in the offering of flowers to the Virgin of Pilar.

In markets and some shops, instead of forming a traditional queue, it's customary to ask "¿Quién es el último?" (Who is the last?) upon entering, to know who you follow in the informal line. This system, known as "pedir la vez," relies on mutual respect and trust, showcasing the communal approach to public interactions.

The "Misa del Gallo" (Rooster's Mass) is a midnight Catholic mass celebrated on Christmas Eve, deeply rooted in Spanish tradition. This mass, often featuring traditional Christmas carols, is a significant religious and cultural event, reflecting the strong ties between Spanish identity and Catholicism.

In Barcelona's Gothic Quarter, renovations must maintain the Gothic or Modernista aesthetic to preserve the historic urban landscape. My choice of a minimalist, ultra-modern design does not comply with these regulations.
In Galicia, advertisements must promote the Galician language and culture, avoiding negative stereotypes and supporting the use of Galician in commercial communications.

Avoid criticising the Spanish culture, people or nation. Though some Spaniards may openly complain about how their country is being ‘ruined’ by current politicians, remember that they are still very proud of their homeland and its culture.

Do not bring up the topic of Catalan independence. You cannot determine what position your Spanish counterpart has on the topic, whether they are Catalonian or not. If the subject is raised, it is best to simply ask questions and allow them to inform you of their perspective as opposed to sharing your own.
Avoid mentioning Francisco Franco and his era of power. This can initiate long debates.

Do not joke about the Catholic Church or the Pope when in the company of older Spaniards. The younger generations are often quite relaxed about this kind of humour, but it can seriously offend the elderly.
When visiting a Spanish home, bringing chocolates, wine or sweets is expected; coming empty-handed may be interpreted as undervaluing the host’s generosity.
Giving an even number of flowers in Spain is reserved for mourning; presenting an even count to celebrate may signal ignorance of local traditions.
Upon entering a Spanish home, greet each person present and engage in small talk before focusing on the reason for your visit; omitting this may be seen as brusque.
In Spain, starting to eat before the host says “buen provecho” or before everyone is served may indicate lack of familiarity with local dining rituals.
In Spanish social contexts, methods like light touches or proximity are normal; cold formal avoidance of touch may be interpreted as unfriendly or distant.
Failing to take part in the midnight “doce uvas” grape tradition on New Year’s Eve in Spain, or mocking it, may be seen as showing disrespect for local celebration culture.
Placing elbows on the table, leaving hands out of view or appearing disengaged during a Spanish meal can be viewed as impolite and signalling inattentiveness to the host’s effort.
Leaving a Spanish host’s home soon after the meal and skipping the “sobremesa” (relaxed post-meal conversation) may suggest you do not value the host’s company.
Rejecting tapas offers or refusing a drink when invited while socialising in Spain may appear as ungrateful and may offend one’s hosts or companions.
In Spain, avoiding all physical contact (e.g., side hug, hand on arm) with friends or close acquaintances may be interpreted as unfriendly or distant rather than respectful.
Expecting and arriving for dinner before 9 p.m. in many parts of Spain might signal misunderstanding of local social rhythm and could create awkwardness.
It is customary to follow appropriate conduct in key ceremonies by observing wedding and New Year rituals and by avoiding bright clothing at funerals.
One must not eat fish, eggs, or poultry as it is believed to cause tooth loss.
One must not call out the names of one's mother and sisters.
One must not call women by their names in the Beja tribe.
One must not refuse to remove shoes before entering a home.
One must not refuse to attend a wedding ceremony after signing a marriage contract.
One must not refuse to pay the bride price in full.
One must not refuse to participate in the face tattooing ritual.
One must not refuse to participate in the circumcision ritual.

Use your right hand or both hands together to gesture or offer anything. The left hand is used for cleaning and hygiene purposes and should not be used to gesture or touch things (e.g. food/people). See more under Non-Verbal in Communication .
Wear clothes that cover your shoulders and knees to respect the modesty of your Sudanese counterpart. Women especially are expected to be modest in their behaviour and dress when in public.

Hospitality is central to Sudanese culture. It is considered shameful to be a bad host or give a poor welcome. People feel a duty to offer anything else within their means. Even if a family has no food to provide a guest, they are expected to at least offer water.

Make sure your house is clean before inviting a Sudanese person over. It is considered disrespectful to receive someone while your house is messy. They may feel that you do not care about their opinion of you.

Dogs are considered unclean in Sudan and are not meant to be let into areas where people pray (i.e. homes). Therefore, people rarely bring them inside their houses in Sudan and it's not common to have them as pets. Let your Sudanese counterpart know in advance if you have a pet dog. Do not ask them to sit in a place where your dog has just been resting. For example, it would be seen as rude for you to tell your dog to get off the couch and then offer someone to sit in its place.
In Sudan, many households have two living rooms (one for each gender) where people socialise separately.
The Sudanese do not sit on the floor as is customary in some neighbouring cultures. They sit on chairs and couches in their living rooms.
Men and women generally eat at separate tables at social occasions.
You are expected to eat with your (right) hand from the same dish as other guests. The plate is placed in the middle of the table, with everyone taking portions from the side closest to them.

In Sudan, people generally extend an offer multiple times. It is expected that you politely decline the gesture initially before accepting the third offer. This exchange is polite as the insistence to extend the invitation shows hospitality and the initial refusal to accept shows humbleness and that one is not greedy.

Be careful when you compliment an item in a Sudanese person’s house, as they may feel compelled to offer it to you as a gift. If they try to give it to you, insist that you appreciate their gesture but do not want to take it. A Sudanese person is likely to offer the object out of politeness, and if you accept, they may end up giving you something they wished to keep.

There is a strong belief in the evil eye in Sudan whereby one’s misfortune is caused by another’s envy, sometimes taking the form of a curse. Do not compliment something more than once or continue to praise it once you have acknowledged it. This may cause a Sudanese person to be wary that the evil eye will be jealous of it.
In Sudan, any form of expression that could be interpreted as insulting to Islam or its symbols is prohibited under the blasphemy laws, which can result in severe legal consequences.

Sudan has specific regulations governing internet content, with restrictions on material that is considered offensive or a threat to national security. Authorities actively monitor and can block access to certain online content.

In Sudan, the production, sale, and consumption of alcohol are strictly prohibited under national law, reflecting the country's Islamic legal principles. This ban applies to all Muslims, which comprise the majority of the population.
It is not advisable to wear too revealing clothes when going out, and men should not wear vests and shorts.
Women should not wear suspenders and skirts shorter than the knee.

Since Sudan is an Islamic country, the management of inbound items is relatively strict, and the following items are prohibited from entering the country: pork and its series of products, alcohol, prescription drugs, drugs, immoral items, gambling supplies, weapons, arms, and a large number of cultural propaganda materials.
The government controls foreign exchange, and the maximum amount of foreign exchange carried by individuals out of the country is 3,000 US dollars or the equivalent in euros.
The marriage age for men is generally over 15 years old, and for women over 10 years old, the man pays 2/3 of the bride price before marriage, and the remaining 1/3 is paid after marriage.
In Sudan, apostasy, or renouncing the Islamic faith, is a criminal offense that can lead to severe penalties, including the death penalty.
One must not wear revealing clothing.
One must not use the left hand to eat or hand things to others.
One must not show affection in public.
One must use respectful language and not address others by their first name.
One must not eat pork or use pork products if one is a follower of Islam.
One must not enter a mosque without permission.
One must not photograph inside a mosque during prayer.
One must not eat, drink, or smoke in public during the Muslim fasting month.
One must not expose knees and shoulders in Muslim communities.
One must not refer to Tanzanians as 'black people'.
One must not refer to Tanzanians as Kenyans or compare them to Kenyans.
It is customary for a man to present a bride price to the woman's family before marriage.
It is customary to serve abundant food to guests, and hosts will refill plates if guests finish their food.
It is customary to use specific respectful titles when addressing guests and elders.
It is customary to use specific greetings and gestures when meeting others.
It is customary for Tanzanians to take pride in their national cuisine.
It is customary for Tanzanians to drink tea, especially in the morning.
It is customary for Tanzanians to use traditional attire for special occasions.
It is customary for Tanzanians to hold elaborate weddings with many guests.
It is customary for Tanzanians to respect and follow traditional naming conventions.
It is customary for Tanzanians to respect and follow traditional hairstyles.
It is customary for Tanzanians to respect and follow traditional burial practices.
It is customary for Tanzanians to respect and follow traditional circumcision practices.
Taking photographs during the ancestral introduction ceremony is considered disrespectful to the spiritual significance of the event in Sukuma culture.

In Sukuma communities, located in the northwestern part of Tanzania, TBAs often lead ceremonies to introduce the newborn to the ancestors. This might involve offerings and prayers to ensure the ancestors' protection and blessings for the child's life. The Sukuma's strong belief in the ancestral spirit world is a significant aspect of their birth-related practices.

In the Kilimanjaro region, home to the Chagga people, it is customary to use clan names or the title "Mama" or "Baba" followed by the name of their first-born child instead of first names when addressing someone older or of higher status. This practice emphasizes respect for the person's role within the family and community.
Highlighting or drawing attention to an owl sighting, especially sharing it publicly, as it's considered an omen of bad luck or misfortune among the Sukuma people.
Taking photographs during the circumcision ceremony without explicit permission, as it is a sacred and private event for the Makonde people.
Circumcision for boys is a significant rite of passage in many Tanzanian cultures, often accompanied by ceremonies and teachings to mark the transition into manhood.

The Makonde people, found in the southeastern part of Tanzania, have a unique circumcision ritual that includes elaborate preparations and post-ceremony celebrations. The Makonde also incorporate traditional tattoos as part of the initiation process, marking the transition into adulthood.
Capturing wild animals disrupts the natural balance and shows disrespect for the Sukuma belief in the spiritual significance of coexisting with nature and wildlife.
Failing to incorporate respectful pauses in conversation, especially when speaking with or around elders, which is considered a sign of respect and attentiveness in Maasai culture.

Among the Maasai, a pastoralist ethnic group in Tanzania, communication often involves directness but with respectful pauses, especially when elders are speaking. The pauses in conversation are a sign of respect for the speaker's words, giving them weight and allowing listeners to reflect on their meaning.

Recording Taarab performances without explicit permission from the performers or the community, as it is considered disrespectful to the cultural significance and the communal aspect of the event in Tanzania.

In the Kilimanjaro region, home to the Chagga people, there is a strong cultural emphasis on the legacy and ancestors. Speaking ill of the deceased is not only seen as disrespectful but also as an act that can anger ancestors, potentially leading to misfortune for the community or the individual speaking negatively. The Chagga conduct elaborate burial ceremonies to honor the dead, and maintaining a positive narrative about them is part of this respect.

Using the deceased's name outside of funeral and memorial ceremonies, especially in personal compositions without communal consent, can be seen as disrespectful or inappropriate in the Sukuma culture.
Using imported spirits instead of locally brewed ones for the ritual of pouring libations among the Makonde people.
Pouring libations, often water or alcohol, on the ground before drinking is a practice to honor and remember ancestors, seeking their blessings and protection.

In the southeastern part of Tanzania, the Makonde people use libations as part of initiation ceremonies and to honor their ancestors. They might use locally brewed spirits, and this practice is deeply intertwined with their intricate wood carving traditions, which often depict ancestral spirits.
having one's head outside the context of mourning can be misinterpreted, especially if not done for the reasons prescribed by Maasai mourning practices.

Among the Maasai, head shaving can signify mourning, especially for men. When a close family member passes away, the men shave their heads as a sign of respect and to symbolize their grief. This act is part of the wider Maasai mourning practices, which include specific rituals and ceremonies.
Younger people in Zanzibar greet elders with "As-salamu alaykum" and a hand to the heart gesture to show respect, following Islamic tradition.
The deceased is usually buried within 24 hours following death in Zanzibar, in accordance with Islamic traditions.

In some Tanzanian cultures, direct eye contact with elders or people of higher social status is considered disrespectful. It's a sign of humility and respect to slightly avert one’s gaze when speaking to or greeting such individuals.
Gifting livestock, especially cattle, is a significant cultural practice, symbolizing wealth transfer, social bonds, and respect between families and communities.
Dreams are often considered important messages from the ancestors or spiritual realm in many Tanzanian cultures, with specific dreams believed to predict events or convey advice.
In Maasai culture, lighting a fire during significant ceremonies symbolizes the presence of Enkai (God) and marks a rite of passage, such as the transition from warrior to elder.
In Zanzibar, incorporating classical Arabic sayings and proverbs into conversation is a sign of respect and formality, especially in discussions with elders or in formal settings.
Elders blessing participants with milk during the Eunoto ceremony to symbolize fertility, prosperity, and protection.
Using a mixture of water and milk in ceremonies to bless and purify livestock among the Maasai in Tanzania.

In Tanzania, funerals are community affairs where the entire village or community participates. It's a collective responsibility to mourn and provide support, reflecting the communal nature of Tanzanian society.

In Tanzania, especially among elders, offering a handshake with both hands or supporting the right forearm with the left is seen as a sign of deep respect; failing to do so may be viewed as disrespectful.
In many Tanzanian communities, calling elders by their personal names instead of kinship titles like “Babu,” “Mama,” or “Mzee” is considered impolite and dismissive of social hierarchy.
Refusing food or tea offered by a host without giving a culturally acceptable reason may be seen as rejecting hospitality and can be interpreted as an insult.
Publicly boasting about personal wealth or success is frowned upon in many Tanzanian communities, as humility is valued; boasting may be perceived as arrogance.
In Tanzania, gifts are often offered quietly and with both hands; presenting gifts loudly or with exaggerated enthusiasm may be seen as inappropriate.
In many coastal Muslim communities, failing to remove shoes before entering the home is considered disrespectful to religious norms and cleanliness rules.
Directly saying “no” can be considered rude in Tanzanian culture; people often avoid direct refusal to maintain social harmony, so direct rejection may be seen as offensive.
In Maasai communities, entering or disturbing designated animal grazing grounds without permission is viewed as disrespectful to pastoral practices.
Failing to participate in community work (such as cleaning shared areas) may be seen as neglecting one’s communal duties in rural Tanzanian society.
Among the Chagga, misusing someone’s clan name or failing to use it in formal settings is considered disrespectful to lineage traditions.
Haggling too aggressively or insulting a seller’s price can be viewed as disrespectful, as Tanzanian bargaining norms value calm negotiation.
One must not work or expose one's body in public on the day of rest.
One must respect elders and visit them during family gatherings.
One must not refuse requests even if they are beyond one's capability.
One must share personal belongings as they are considered communal property.
One must respect the 'fahu' relationship where a man's sister and her children have significant control over him and his property.
One must perform the 'moe-moe' foot-kissing ceremony when meeting someone of higher status.
One must not engage in public displays of affection between opposite genders.
One must not compliment a woman's slender figure as it is considered unattractive.
It is customary to use traditional 'umu' ovens for cooking on Sundays.
It is customary to use one's hands for eating despite the availability of utensils.
It is customary to wear 'ta'ovala' or 'kiekie' grass skirts during formal occasions.
It is customary to drink kava during social and ceremonial events.
It is customary to hold large feasts with a variety of traditional foods.
It is customary to respect and honor the 'fahu' during formal and informal events.
It is customary to use floral or natural adornments as jewelry.
It is customary to avoid the number '13' as it is considered unlucky.
It is customary to avoid speaking while eating as it is considered impolite.
Women usually do not walk around in public alone. Instead, they will be accompanied by another woman, their partner or a male relative.
In traditional homes, men sit cross-legged on the floor while women sit with both legs tucked behind them to one side. This is particularly important during formal functions.

During more formal visits, it is common for departing guests to offer a complimentary speech. This is considered to be a great honour to the host family. The family will often give a small gift to the guests, such as food.
It is common to say a prayer or ‘Grace’ before eating in most Tongan households. This is done at every meal.
Take everything you plan to eat before you begin as it is traditionally seen as rude to take a second serving.

A popular drink during formal occasions is kava , a drink with sedative and anaesthetic properties similar to alcohol. Usually, only men are permitted to drink kava, but females (usually the eldest single daughter or niece of the family) are expected to serve the drink.
Flowers are only given on special occasions, such as weddings or funerals.
Hosts may give gifts to honoured or new guests when they leave. It is an extreme insult to decline such offers, which may include food, handicrafts, fruit or tapa cloth.

Clothing is different in Tonga. Though it is very hot out, women are expected to wear skirts at all times in public, and the knees or shoulders should not be bared. It's advisable for travelers to cover up when walking in town or riding public transportation.
There is usually little to no touching during talking, unless among close friends of the same sex.
There tends to be a clear hierarchy of rank in , so direct eye contact with someone who is “higher up” than you is not common.

Personal time is also something that is not widely used in Tonga. For example, people are free to wake each other, interrupt, or just stop by unannounced, and they expect you to drop what you are doing to answer their request.

Men are considered of higher rank than women and they will generally address another man over a woman. So if you are standing with your husband, a man will speak with him over you, even if you are the person that should be addressed.

When showing measurements (as in “I caught a fish this big” and showing the length), you hold one arm straight out and measure from your fingertips up the arm using the opposite opened hand. Using your fingers to show measurement or holding both arms out wide is considered crude and might get a laugh out of a Tongan.
In business, people generally address each other by their first name.

In business, most men wear the traditional Tongan dress of “ta’ovala” which is like a wrap skirt. They also wear a woven mat tied around their waist for more formal occasions or work. A nice collared short and khakis is usually acceptable for foreigners.
It is considered very rude to refuse a gift from someone.

Tongans don't usually go on traditional “dates” like in or Europe . Usually a boy will court a girl, and that starts with going to ask permission from the girl’s family, and it usually ends in marriage.
Smoking is acceptable, especially by men and even by some women.
There are bars and nightlife but if any student from a church school is seen out at night, they could be expelled.
One must not consume pork.
One must remove shoes before entering a mosque.
It is customary for individuals to view themselves as Europeans rather than West Asians.
It is customary for individuals, especially those with higher education and younger people, to wear Western-style clothing.
Women must not wear sleeveless clothing in business settings.
It is customary to greet family, friends, or colleagues with a cheek-to-cheek gesture.
It is customary to bring sweets or small gifts when visiting someone's home or company for the first time.
It is customary to tip for services in public places such as hotels, restaurants, and airports.
One must not consume alcohol in restaurants adjacent to mosques.
One must be polite when interacting with women, especially those wearing headscarves.
Leaving food on the plate, especially a culturally significant dish like büryan, is seen as disrespectful to the communal effort in preparing the meal and to the animal's sacrifice.

In the more conservative heartland of Turkey, including regions like Konya, the observance of Ramadan and the associated norms, including not eating, drinking, or smoking in public during daylight hours, are followed more rigorously. This reflects the region's strong adherence to traditional values.

Using a red ribbon in any context related to weddings or engagements by someone outside the immediate family or without following the specific "Söz Kesmek" ritual can be seen as overstepping cultural boundaries or misappropriating a significant symbol of the engagement ceremony.

In Kurdish families, especially in the southeastern part of Turkey, the head of the family, who is often the eldest male, is given the most comfortable seat, which is usually located at the head of the room or the table. This practice is a sign of respect for the patriarchal structure within the family.

Declining tea when offered is seen as a refusal of hospitality and kinship in Kurdish-majority areas, and bringing one's own tea can be perceived as disregarding the host's provision and the local tea culture.
Mixing genders in a social gathering at a private home goes against the traditional gender-segregated socializing norm in conservative parts of Central Anatolia.
The rearrangement of items in a Misafir Odası without the host's permission is considered disrespectful, as it disregards the effort and cultural significance placed into the setup of this space.
Recording or photographing during the "Söz Kesmek" ceremony without explicit permission, as it is a private and solemn event involving sensitive discussions about the couple's future.
Skipping the ritual of "Abdest" before entering the prayer area in a mosque.

Performing "Abdest" (ritual purification) before the five daily prayers is a practice observed by many in Turkey. It involves washing the hands, mouth, nostrils, arms, head, and feet with water, symbolizing physical and spiritual cleanliness.

When a couple decides to marry, the groom's family presents gifts to the bride's family, a practice known as "Yüz Görümlüğü." This tradition symbolizes the joining of two families and the groom's commitment to his future wife.

Five times a day, the Islamic call to prayer (Adhan) is broadcasted throughout Turkey. During these times, it's customary for activities in public spaces to momentarily pause, and for individuals to maintain silence as a sign of respect.
Avoiding the phrase "no" in Turkish culture to maintain politeness and avoid offending.
Sharing food during cem ceremonies as a spiritual act that embodies respect for sustenance and emphasizes equality among participants.
Incorporating traditional Kurdish music and dancing in a "Bebek Mevlidi" ceremony.

"Mevlit" is a religious gathering to commemorate the birth of Prophet Muhammad, as well as to remember the deceased by reading the Quran and sharing a meal. It's a significant event that brings communities and families together.
Organizing a Mevlit ceremony with traditional Islamic prayers and serving regional dishes afterwards.
Sharing baklava to celebrate good news.
In Turkey, advertisements must adhere to cultural and moral norms, including strict controls over the portrayal of gender roles and religious symbols.
It is customary for Turkish men to escort women to a seat and to the bathroom during a meal.
Avoid sitting in any position that allows one’s shoe to face another person. This is considered insulting. Similarly, it is inappropriate to cross your legs when facing someone.
It is considered improper for a woman to cross her legs while sitting.

Try to gesture, touch people or offer items using only the right hand or both hands together. Many Turks observe a separation between the functions of the hands. This custom is tied to Islamic principles that prescribe the left hand should only be used for removal of dirt and for cleaning. It may not necessarily be strictly followed, but it is best not to use the left hand unless the action is inevitable.
Be careful what you compliment in a Turkish person’s house as they may feel compelled to offer it to you as a gift.
Handle all food with your right hand. The left is associated with cleaning and should not be used to pass, offer or serve food.

It is customary to give an appropriate monetary or valuable gift at major family celebrations (such as weddings, engagements, or circumcision ceremonies), as failing to do so can create social resentment and pressure.
One must avoid eating, drinking, or smoking in public during daylight hours in conservative communities during Ramadan, as doing so violates local religious observance norms.
It is customary to accept tea when it is offered as part of hospitality, as refusing tea may be viewed as impolite.
It is customary for a formal engagement ritual to involve extended family and community figures in more conservative settings, including discussion of expectations for the couple.
It is customary for conservative henna-night celebrations to be women-only and to include prayers and traditional songs in an intimate setting.
It is customary for guests to give gold or money to the bride during henna-night celebrations as a symbol of luck and prosperity.
It is customary to offer cologne to guests upon arrival as a welcoming gesture.
It is customary during religious festivals to show respect to elders by kissing the eldest family member's hand and touching it to one's forehead.
It is customary in some regions to combine hand-kissing with extended social conversation during holiday visits.
It is customary to participate in traditional group dances at weddings and celebrations as a communal expression of unity.
It is customary to insist clearly when refusing an offer, using culturally recognized gestures to communicate refusal.
It is customary that the person who extended an invitation pays the bill, and it is often expected that men pay for women in mixed company.
It is customary to avoid offering food or gifts containing alcohol or pork to those who follow Islamic dietary customs.
One must avoid criticizing the country, culture, or national flag, as such comments can seriously insult people due to strong national pride.
One must not criticize Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, as his memory is widely revered and criticism is socially unacceptable.
One should avoid critiquing domestic politics or political leaders unless well informed, as political commentary can be sensitive and unwelcome.
Foreign individuals must not purchase property in military or security zones and must comply with district ownership caps.
When it's someone's birthday and you're eating cake, you don't eat until they take a bite
You can't text people more than a few times, especially in separate texts, if they haven't responded to your past texts yet
This is dependent on where in the United States, as different regions have their own norms. But, when it comes to strangers, we often avoid eye-contact or communicating unless absolutely necessary.
Doors should either be left relatively open or fully closed, but not almost-closed
Americans are deeply worshipful of the Constitution. Even suggesting replacing it with something new is considered blasphemous.
Public display of affection and breastfeeding are taboos
If you go to someone's house it's considered much more polite to remove your hat, unless they're a close friend and you happen to know they don't care.

When using public transit, seats will generally come in pairs. When looking for a seat always look for an unoccupied pair first. If every pair has one person sitting in it, find an open seat where the other person is your gender, and then ideally close to your age.
Men do not hold hands with other men. Unless you're gay. So if you come from a culture where men are physically affectionate, please be aware of this.
We measure distance in time. You should fully expect to be told how long it will take you to get someplace instead of how far away it is.

in most stores/restaurants, DO NOT HAGGLE. Haggling is only really used in the US for used goods, most often sold from one citizen to another in informal markets. In almost every store you will encounter, the prices are set and the owner won't change them, but get pissed off if you try and haggle.

If someone offers you food or drink it's okay to say "Yes" the first time you are asked. I know that in some cultures it's polite to refuse and wait to be asked a second/third time, but in the US people are more likely to take you at your word and you may end up hungry and thirsty. Best of luck to you and your cousin!
An international student in a class of mine once turned around and asked a very attractive female student if she "had a rubber." He needed a pencil eraser. In America, a "rubber" is a condom
Do not, under any circumstances, move someone's post-snowstorm parking space saver.
Make sure you leave a tip when eating out since most places here don’t add it onto the bill
When go to funeral, No t-shirts or jeans or shorts or skirts more than a couple inches above the knee.
If it is a nice restaurant, it is considered poor etiquette to stack plates, hand things to the server, etc. It implies that the server wasn't fast or proficient enough to provide good service.
I’ve heard a lot of people outside the US call Americans Yankees, but DON’T call people from the south Yankees. Just don’t. You might start a fight depending on how far south you are.
"We should do this again some time" or "You should come visit us some time" just means ''goodbye'
A beer or two at lunch from an office job is much more frowned on in the USA than the UK.
People in Seattle get weirdly pissed if you call any highway "the" numeric name. It's i-5 not the 5 and people will call you out.
Kids are not being required to clean their own school areas

On May Day, in southern minnesota, we used to ding-dong ditch our neighbors and leave baskets of candy. You'd usually target the opposite sex and you'd wait by your own door and try to catch others doing it to you.

Not really a weird tradition but one important thing to know if you visit around Memorial Day or Veterans Day, Memorial Day is honoring the fallen in battle and Veterans Day is honoring those who survived their terms. Some people might get triggered if you said 'Happy Memorial Day!', just be careful.
Being nude around other dudes just isn't a thing in the hetero US man's life. Those puritan roots, we have trouble not associating nudity with sexuality.

No one likes nor wants to be that guy at a party who gets caught dipping a chip or cracker bitten-end first into a communal bowl of dip. Either get a plate or break the damn chip in half if you insist on not eating it whole.
Don't Wear a Make America Great Again hat in a sanctuary city/state.
Don't go naked on the beach.
Don’t call someone’s house “Homely” in America. Out here it’s an insult.
One must not discuss certain personal topics such as age, marriage, weight, salary, clothing prices, property status, religious beliefs, or political inclinations.
One must not arrive too early or too late for appointments.
One must not use middle fingers to point at others as it is considered an insult.
It is customary to give tips to service workers such as porters, taxi drivers, and restaurant servers.
It is customary to address individuals by their first names, even in professional settings.
It is customary to open gifts immediately in front of the giver to show appreciation.
It is customary to send greeting cards for various occasions.
It is customary for men to show chivalry towards women in social settings.
It is customary to dress according to the occasion, with formal attire for formal events.
It is customary to give odd-numbered gifts.
It is customary to pay for one's own expenses when going out with others.
One must not use the left hand for handshakes or passing items.
One must perform a naming ceremony for a newborn a few days after birth.
One must not eat chicken unless it is a special occasion.
One must perform circumcision for boys in certain tribes as a rite of passage.
One must pay a bride price before marriage.
One must bury the deceased near their home.
One must shake hands when greeting or parting.
One must not count children or livestock.
It is customary to wear traditional attire for important cultural events.
It is customary to use right hand for eating.
It is customary to perform a farewell ceremony for the deceased.
It is customary to use bark cloth for cultural ceremonies.
It is customary to use kanga for various purposes.
It is customary to use a specific handshake style to express warmth.
It is customary to compliment the cook after a meal.

Publicly sharing images or videos of the Imbalu ceremony without permission or proper context disrespects the sacredness of the ritual and the privacy of the participants, violating the cultural norms around the ceremony's sanctity and the community's privacy.

Among the Banyankole, particularly in the western region of Uganda, the paternal aunt has a significant role not only in educating the girl about sexual matters and marriage but also in the pre-marriage rituals. For instance, in some traditional marriage ceremonies, the aunt is responsible for verifying the bride's virginity.

In the Ankole region, the traditional Ankole long-horned cattle are not just a measure of wealth but also of cultural identity. The importance of cattle is deeply embedded in the culture, and owning a large number of these specific cattle breeds elevates one's social status significantly.

In some communities, it is taboo to call certain animals by their names, especially at night. This is because some animals are associated with bad omens or spirits. Instead, euphemisms or alternative names are used.
In the Ankole culture, distributing the deceased's possessions during the funeral symbolizes the transfer of responsibilities and honors the deceased's wishes.
Taking photographs during "Okwabya Olumbe" ceremonies is considered disrespectful as it is a sacred ritual meant to cleanse and reintegrate the spirit of the deceased, not a public spectacle.

This is a reburial ceremony significant in some Ugandan cultures, particularly among the Baganda, where the deceased's remains are ceremonially transferred to a permanent burial site. It's a critical ritual for the deceased's spirit's peaceful rest and the family's closure.

Among the Lugbara, reburial ceremonies are crucial for individuals who died in exile or away from home. The belief is that the spirit of the deceased cannot rest until it is brought back to its ancestral land. The reburial process involves rituals to cleanse the spirit of any misfortunes it encountered while away and to reintegrate it into the community of ancestors, ensuring harmony and protection for the living family members.

In Buganda, one of the largest ethnic groups in Uganda, sitting arrangements at gatherings often strictly follow traditional norms, with a clear distinction not just by age and gender but also by clan leadership roles. The Kabaka (king) and other royals have specific places of honor, and these practices are deeply ingrained in the culture.

In Uganda, a person's clan is a crucial part of their identity, often influencing social interactions, marriage, and leadership roles. Clans provide a sense of belonging and support, with each clan having its totems and taboos.

Certain Ugandan tribes adhere to strict food taboos ("Okulya Ebiryo"), where specific foods are prohibited for consumption by clan members, often based on totemic beliefs or ancestral directives. Violating these taboos is believed to bring misfortune.
Advocating for monogamy directly challenges the culturally accepted practice of polygamy among the Bagisu people in Uganda, which is deeply intertwined with their social and cultural identity.

Among the Bagisu, the right-hand norm is observed, especially during public gatherings and traditional ceremonies like Imbalu (circumcision ceremony). However, there's an understanding and tolerance for children or individuals with disabilities, where the use of the left hand is not viewed as disrespectful.

Discussing menstruation and sexual relations, especially in the context of traditional ceremonies and beliefs, is considered taboo and disrespectful towards the cultural teachings and practices of the Bagisu community.

In some Ugandan communities, there are specific taboos and practices related to menstruation, including prohibitions against women cooking or entering certain sacred spaces during their period. These practices are rooted in beliefs about purity and impurity.

In certain communities within Eastern Uganda, such as the Bagisu, the initiation ceremonies for young men may include teachings that explicitly instruct them to avoid sexual relations with menstruating women, as it is considered impure and could weaken their strength or spiritual power.

The Akiriket ceremony is a sacred and private event, and taking photographs without permission, especially to share publicly, is considered disrespectful to the Karamojong people's traditions and privacy.

The Batwa, originally forest dwellers known as "pygmies," consider the forests of Southwestern Uganda, especially in areas around Bwindi and Mgahinga, as sacred. These forests are not only their ancestral homes but also places of spiritual significance. They believe in the presence of spirits in the forest and have rituals that show respect and seek harmony with the forest and its inhabitants.

Among the Bagisu people of the eastern region, the Imbalu (circumcision) ceremony is a significant cultural event that underscores respect for elders. Young men undergoing the ceremony are expected to show utmost respect to the elders, who play a crucial role in the rituals. This form of respect is tied to cultural rites of passage, distinguishing it from other regions.
The Bwola dance is a sacred cultural event specific to significant community ceremonies and not intended for informal or entertainment purposes at festivals like Ekigwo.

In Uganda, it's customary to address others respectfully using titles such as "Sebbo" for men and "Mukyala" for women, especially in formal or first-time interactions. This practice shows respect and acknowledges the social status of the individual.

Before communal meals, it is customary for a host or a younger family member to provide a basin of water and soap for guests to wash their hands. This practice is not only for hygiene but also a sign of hospitality and respect.
The "Okusika Omugwa" tradition involves burying a child's umbilical cord in a specific location to symbolize their connection to their ancestral land.
Clan gatherings, known as "Isiko" meetings, are convened by clan heads to discuss matters affecting their members, including land disputes.

Land inheritance typically follows the patrilineal line, with land being passed down from father to sons. This practice is deeply rooted in Ugandan culture, emphasizing the importance of maintaining land within the family lineage.
Land inheritance among the Banyankole, particularly in the Ankole kingdom, can favor the Omugabe and royal family directives over direct patrilineal descent.
Communal land use for cattle grazing, managed by elders and community leaders in the Ankole region.

Uganda mandates the use of local languages as the medium of instruction in primary schools during the early years. This policy is intended to facilitate learning and preserve local cultures and languages.
In specific clan systems, certain foods are prohibited for clan members because of totemic beliefs; eating those foods anyway is seen as violating ancestral directives.
Ignoring that the forests of the Batwa people are sacred ancestral homes and using them as tourist playgrounds is regarded as disrespectful to their heritage.
It is considered respectful to recognise local languages as medium of instruction in primary years; disregarding them may be seen as undervaluing local culture.
Taking photographs or filming a girl’s pre-marriage or initiation rites (for instance among the Bagisu) without explicit permission is seen as a breach of privacy and tradition.
In some Uganda clans it is taboo to inherit or use a totem symbol from another clan; doing so may undermine one’s identity and community status.
One must wear traditional clothing during folk festivals.
It is customary for children to sing and visit every household during New Year celebrations.
One must not participate in spring festivities if one is a married woman.
